Real Madrid beat RB Leipzig 3-1 in the Champions League on Tuesday, extending their momentum in the competition with a home victory that featured goals from multiple attackers and a steady defensive performance.

Real took the lead after establishing control in midfield and pressing Leipzig in advanced areas. The hosts broke the game open with a precise finish that put them in front, forcing the visitors to take more risks as the match progressed. Leipzig responded by creating openings through transitions and set pieces, but Real’s ability to absorb pressure and win second balls limited their chances.

Leipzig equalised to level the score, capitalising on moments when Real’s line was stretched and turning pressure into a direct, clinical threat. The equaliser shifted the tempo and briefly brought Leipzig into the rhythm of the match, with both sides trading attacks in the closing stages of the first half and early in the second.

Real regained the advantage in the second half with a goal that came after sustained pressure near the Leipzig area. The strike restored the hosts’ lead and gave them a clearer path to manage the game, with Madrid pushing forward while maintaining structure behind the ball. Leipzig continued to seek an equaliser, but their attempts were repeatedly interrupted by Real’s defensive cover and disciplined ball recoveries.

The decisive moment arrived when Real added a third goal, sealing the win with a finish that reflected their increased efficiency in front of goal. Leipzig had chances to reduce the deficit again, but Real’s goalkeeper and back line held firm, defending crosses and clearing danger in dangerous positions.

After the match, Real Madrid coach Carlo Ancelotti said the team’s performance was defined by “the balance between being brave with the ball and being ready when we had to defend,” adding that “we made key decisions at important moments.”

With the result, Real Madrid moved closer to the next stage of the tournament, while Leipzig will look to regroup ahead of their remaining fixtures. The two sides next meet in the return leg, where the outcome is likely to be shaped by how Leipzig manage Real’s pressing and how Madrid handle the counterattacks that featured in Tuesday’s game.

So it’s March and you’re staring at that leftover compost like it personally betrayed you. Been there. The weather’s doing that annoying “maybe spring, maybe frost, who knows” thing, and suddenly you don’t want to just dump your compost everywhere and hope for the best.

Here’s what I’ve been doing with leftover compost in March (when things are warming up but the ground might still be weirdly cold).

1. **Top-dress established plants**
If you already have beds with perennials, shrubs, or even garlic/leeks that you planted last fall, you can add a thin layer of compost on top. I aim for about 1/2 inch. Don’t bury stems or crowns—compost is not mulch pizza. This helps kickstart microbial activity as temperatures rise.

2. **Use it like “starter” for new planting holes**
For things you’re planting now (spring greens, peas, early flowers), mix compost into the top part of the soil, not deep down. I usually blend it with the existing soil at roughly 1:3 compost to native soil, then plant as normal. It gives roots a friendly environment without turning your bed into a soggy sponge.

3. **Make compost tea (if you’re impatient)**
If the compost is fully finished and not full of recognizable stuff, you can steep it for a quick soil boost. I’m not going to pretend it’s magic, but it’s better than letting compost sit in a bin while you procrastinate. (Also, don’t use gross, half-baked compost for this—it should smell earthy, not like a science experiment.)

4. **Store it smart if you’re not ready yet**
If you don’t have a spot or you’re waiting for the ground to warm, cover it tightly with a tarp or use a bin lid. Moisture matters. You want it to stay slightly damp, not turn into a dried-out brick.

One thing: if your compost isn’t fully “finished,” I’d avoid using it directly on seedbeds. I learned that the hard way—tiny seedlings don’t care about your intentions, they care about stability.

What kind of compost are you working with right now—finished, semi-finished, or still a bit chunky—and do you have beds already set up?

Y Combinator is once again trying to put guardrails around the chaos—this time with a dedicated AI-safety track for its W26 batch. On paper, it’s a sensible move: if startups are going to build models that can write code, draft persuasive text, and optimize decision-making, then the ecosystem needs a runway for thinking about misuse, evaluation, and responsible deployment. In practice, the move also signals something more pointed: VC-friendly “move fast and break things” is quietly running out of patience when the things being broken are human trust, privacy, and public safety.

The track arrives as the startup world is racing toward increasingly capable AI systems—often with incomplete answers about how those systems behave under pressure. YC’s new curriculum reportedly focuses on safety engineering and measurement rather than vague ethics posters. That means more attention to red-teaming, failure modes, and model behavior under adversarial or high-stakes conditions, alongside guidance on how to translate safety work into product timelines. The framing matters. It treats safety as a build requirement, not a compliance afterthought, which is exactly what teams need if they want to ship responsibly without grinding to a halt.

Still, it’s hard not to view this as partially defensive PR—because the demand for “AI safety” has already started to harden into external pressure, from regulators and customers to internal risk committees at enterprises. YC’s influence is enormous, and when it signals priorities, founders scramble to align. The real question is whether the track results in measurable improvements across the graduating class, or whether it simply creates a new checklist of deliverables that look good on demo day.

If YC pulls this off, it could help set a standard for how early-stage companies should validate safety claims, not just marketing promises. And if it doesn’t, the backlash will be swift: the next wave of AI incidents will not care whether a startup attended a “safety track.” The next year will show whether W26 becomes a template for responsible AI shipping—or just another signal that founders are chasing the right buzzwords before the next regulation lands.

The NBA’s trade deadline on Tuesday closed a busy week of roster moves that reshaped contender depth charts while leaving some teams unable to make major improvements, league sources said.

Several teams used the deadline to strengthen key areas as the regular season entered its final stretch. Denver added frontcourt options aimed at improving matchups in late-game rotations, according to a person familiar with the deal terms. The move, which included salary matching and draft compensation, was designed to give the Nuggets more size and rebounding when opponents pressed small lineups. Executives involved said the additions also increased flexibility around injuries and foul-prone minutes for rotation bigs.

In the East, Milwaukee finalized an agreement that added scoring depth, with the Bucks targeting improved perimeter creation off the bench. Team officials said the trade reflected an emphasis on maintaining scoring balance when starters rest. The acquisition also increased Milwaukee’s ability to rotate defenders across multiple positions, a factor that has driven recent playoff game plans.

Boston and Oklahoma City both made moves intended to address specific matchup concerns. Boston strengthened its wing depth, hoping to maintain defensive structure against teams that use high-volume shooters. Oklahoma City, sources said, pursued guard play that can help sustain offensive pace in second-unit stretches and reduce reliance on any single primary ballhandler.

Among the players affected, some prominent names changed teams late, increasing uncertainty about early chemistry. Teams said they were focused on integrating newcomers quickly, but coaching staffs acknowledged that playbook adjustments and defensive reads can take time. League scheduling also means several of the first games after the deadline will feature playoff-caliber opponents.

Not all deadline activity produced clear momentum. Teams that did not move for top targets cited internal evaluations that existing rosters still had a path to contend, while others said the price for elite players was too high relative to projected returns. Some organizations also left deals for later, prioritizing development or keeping cap space available for the offseason.

One general manager, speaking on condition of anonymity because multiple executives were coordinating across the league, said the deadline often comes down to whether the cost of a player aligns with the role the team needs most. “You can’t just chase talent,” the executive said. “The question is whether the addition fits the rotation and the timelines.”

Overall, the deadline reshuffled rosters across both conferences, setting up a final stretch in which eligibility, health and on-court fit will determine how quickly the new players can translate trades into results.

People always ask why Byzantium “survived” while the West basically face-planted over a few centuries, and the honest answer is: it’s not one magic difference, it’s a stack of advantages plus better decision-making (and a bit of luck). I’m not saying the Byzantines were flawless—Constantinople still got walloped in wars—but compared to the Western successor states, their whole system just stayed coherent longer.

First big thing: geography and resources. The Eastern Empire had a defensible capital with crazy logistics. Constantinople sat on major trade routes and could be supplied by sea even when land corridors got cut. The West had more fragmented supply lines and, crucially, fewer “fallback” centers of wealth once things started breaking.

Second: the state apparatus actually kept functioning. The Byzantines had a strong bureaucracy and a tax base that (most of the time) kept paying soldiers and maintaining fortifications. In the West, once centralized collection collapsed, a lot of regions basically lived on local arrangements. That works until you’re fighting coordinated external pressure—then it goes sideways fast.

Third: military adaptation. People love to meme about “decline,” but Byzantium kept evolving. They shifted tactics, reorganized forces, and eventually leaned into systems like the themes (regional military administration). Meanwhile, Western polities often relied on federate arrangements and ad hoc levies—again, not always bad, but less resilient when the political map keeps changing overnight.

Fourth: diplomacy and selective stability. Byzantium could play neighbors against each other more effectively because it remained the heavyweight hub. When you can bribe, subsidize, negotiate truces, and still hold your core territory, you buy time. Time is basically the only resource history ever really cares about.

If you want the “one sentence” version I’d give in a comment: the Eastern Empire had a survivable geography, a functioning fiscal-bureaucratic machine, and a military that kept changing—so when the West fragmented, Byzantium could absorb shocks rather than disintegrate.

Anyway, what do you think matters most here—economics, military flexibility, or just that Byzantium had a better capital and fewer catastrophic breaks at once?

So I’m in that “new gardener panic” phase where I’m reading everything and still somehow doubting my own decisions. One of the biggest debates I keep running into is raised beds vs in-ground, and honestly? I think people make it way more complicated than it has to be.

Here’s my take after digging through a bunch of threads and (literal) soil checking: raised beds are the “buy once, cry once” option if your ground is questionable or you’re impatient (same). If your native soil is rocky, compacted, full of weeds, or just plain unknown, raised beds let you control the growing environment without a whole season of rehab. And if you’re the kind of person who doesn’t love wrestling with backbreaking digging… yeah, that matters.

In-ground, though, can be great if you’ve already got decent soil and you don’t mind the work. It’s often cheaper long-term (no lumber, no fill hauled in, no raised-bed obsession). Also, once you’re in, the plants can access a deeper soil profile, which can help during hot dry spells—assuming the soil is actually healthy.

What I’d do if I were starting again (and I’m kinda starting again, but with better notes this time) is ask myself these questions:

1. **How bad is my soil?** (If you don’t know, do a soil test or at least dig a few holes and look.)
2. **How much time do I have?** Raised beds can skip the “let’s amend everything everywhere” phase.
3. **Do I want easier weed management?** Raised beds usually win because the weeds can be contained.
4. **Do I care about accessibility?** If bending hurts or you don’t want to kneel for hours, beds are a lifesaver.

My personal bias: I went with raised beds for the first year because I wanted quick wins and less chaos. I’d rather learn how to grow tomatoes while standing upright than spend months turning clay into “rich, fluffy dream soil” (even though that’s also a fun fantasy). Later, I can always expand into in-ground once I actually understand my land.

What’s your situation—are you going raised beds, in-ground, or a mix—and what made you choose?

The Biden administration has proposed new U.S. liquefied natural gas export rules aimed at tightening how long-term LNG authorizations are issued and monitored, according to a draft proposal reviewed by multiple energy stakeholders.

The changes would update requirements for developers seeking long-term export approval, including additional reporting on planned volumes and infrastructure milestones, as well as clearer timelines for when facilities must reach operational benchmarks. The draft would also expand the circumstances under which authorizations could be modified or require further review, according to people familiar with the proposal.

Under current policy, U.S. approvals for LNG exports—granted through permits that rely on factors such as market need and supply—are governed largely by existing Department of Energy guidance and past authorizations. The proposed rules seek to reduce uncertainty around how much natural gas will be exported over time and to ensure that export projects track their earlier commitments, the sources said.

The Department of Energy would also adjust how it assesses applications, including how it evaluates whether proposed export quantities are consistent with domestic supply and demand forecasts. The draft would place greater emphasis on whether projects can demonstrate financing, permitting progress, and engineering readiness during the pre-construction phase, stakeholders said.

Industry groups said the proposal could affect project schedules, particularly for developers that are still finalizing engineering or negotiating long-term contracts. Several companies have already delayed or scaled back LNG projects amid shifting global prices and competition, and they have sought clearer guidance on how export authorization conditions might be updated during construction.

“Predictability matters for LNG investment, and any new rules should provide a clear, workable framework for applicants and allow projects to proceed based on defined milestones,” said one trade association official who reviewed the draft proposal.

The proposed rules are expected to be published in the Federal Register for public comment, according to the sources. The comment period would give utilities, pipeline operators, LNG developers, and other stakeholders time to submit feedback. The administration has not indicated when a final version would take effect.

If adopted, the rules could influence pending LNG applications and amendments to existing authorizations. They would also shape future reviews by setting more standardized expectations for developers seeking to expand U.S. export capacity, which has grown rapidly in recent years as new terminals began operations.
9cf4877c9d0b6c3aa0e8:
The U.S. Senate on Monday began debate on legislation that would extend a federal tax credit for “clean electricity,” a move supporters say is intended to sustain investment in renewable and other low-emissions power while negotiations continue over the scope and pace of energy transition.

The debate centers on whether to extend the existing credit framework that provides incentives for electricity generation technologies deemed clean under federal rules, including wind, solar, nuclear and certain qualifying projects. If passed, the extension would keep the credits available for new projects beyond the current timeline set by existing law, avoiding a gap that industry groups have warned could delay construction and financing decisions.

Senators also weighed amendments that would adjust eligibility, alter compliance standards, or change the credit’s interaction with other federal and state energy programs. Some proposals target developers’ ability to meet carbon-related requirements, while others focus on how projects qualify when power is sold through different contracting arrangements, such as long-term power purchase agreements.

Lawmakers are debating the bill against a backdrop of ongoing efforts to advance broader clean energy policy, including disputes over which technologies should qualify and how to measure emissions over a project’s life. Supporters argue the credits are necessary to help build firm and renewable generation capacity, particularly in regions seeking to meet electricity demand growth and emissions goals.

Opponents have raised concerns about the cost of extending the credit, as well as the potential for subsidies to overlap with other incentives. They have also questioned whether the credit design has sufficiently tightened to reflect emissions performance and grid reliability considerations.

“Extending this credit is about keeping the lights on and keeping investment moving in the states that are already building clean generation,” a senior Senate sponsor said during floor remarks, according to the senator’s office.

The measure’s chances in the chamber depend on whether Democrats can assemble enough support to overcome objections from members seeking changes to the credit’s eligibility and oversight mechanisms. Senate leaders have indicated that votes could proceed after the completion of amendments, with timing expected to determine whether the bill clears the chamber before scheduled legislative priorities later this month.

The Federal Energy Regulatory Commission on Tuesday approved reforms aimed at reshaping the way new generation projects are screened and processed in the interconnection queue, according to the agency and federal filings.

The order establishes changes at the federal level intended to improve the sequence and timing of interconnection requests, including new requirements for developers to provide more information earlier in the process and clearer timelines for queue withdrawals and study progress. FERC said the reforms are designed to reduce delays and uncertainty that can occur when projects that cannot move forward remain in the queue for extended periods.

Under the decision, the agency directed updates to the pro forma interconnection procedures used by transmission providers, including adjustments to how queues are managed during the clustering of study phases. The revisions also address how requests are grouped for system impact studies and how withdrawn applications are handled, seeking to limit the extent to which large numbers of projects can slow the progress of others seeking to connect.

FERC said it will require transmission providers to implement the revised procedures through their tariff filings, which are expected to be submitted within set compliance deadlines. The commission also outlined enforcement mechanisms tied to adherence to the updated process, including potential remedies if timelines are not met.

Commissioner Mark Christie, in a statement, said the order reflects a “data-driven approach” to queue management and provides “more transparency” for both grid planners and market participants. He said the reforms aim to ensure that interconnection studies are conducted efficiently for projects that are most able to proceed.

The changes come as utilities, developers and consumer groups have argued over the causes of long interconnection backlogs, which can span years due to study complexity, transmission constraints and coordination across regional systems. While FERC has previously issued a series of reforms intended to improve generator access, Tuesday’s action consolidates and updates elements of the interconnection process that apply nationally.

The commission said it expects the updated procedures to affect pending and future interconnection requests, and it directed staff to continue monitoring the implementation of the new rules. Transmission providers will have to revise their tariff language to align with the order, and affected parties are expected to submit comments during compliance review periods.
79c731e55b042727d990:
California has updated the design of its statewide cap-and-trade program, refining how emissions allowances are allocated, monitored and enforced as the state prepares for the next compliance cycle, according to state documents released this week.

The changes were set out in updated regulations and supporting materials from the California Air Resources Board (CARB), the agency that administers the program. The revisions focus on the mechanics of allowance distribution to capped industries, the treatment of auction-related costs, and additional requirements for reporting and verification, CARB said.

Under California’s program, companies in major emitting sectors—primarily electricity generation, large industrial sources and certain transportation fuels—must hold allowances to cover their greenhouse gas emissions. Each year, the cap on allowable emissions declines, and allowances are provided through a combination of auctions and other allocation methods established in law.

Among the most notable updates are adjustments to compliance instruments used by entities participating in the market. CARB said it revised provisions governing how allowances can be transferred and retired, and clarified timelines for submitting compliance reports. The agency also updated certain definitions used to determine eligibility for specific allocation categories, according to the text of the regulatory package.

CARB’s materials also address oversight and enforcement. The updated design includes refinements to monitoring, reporting and verification requirements, including expectations for third-party verification of emissions data submitted by covered entities. The agency said it will continue to conduct audits and other compliance checks to ensure reporting accuracy and alignment with the program’s cap-and-trade rules.

The revisions come as California continues to implement state climate policies intended to reduce emissions and support cleaner energy. CARB said the program remains subject to ongoing public oversight, with staff required to apply the rules consistently and to maintain an emissions-tracking system tied to allowance issuance, auctioning and retirement.

A CARB spokesperson said in a statement that the updates were intended to “improve clarity and operational consistency for market participants” while maintaining the integrity of the cap-and-trade system.

The updated regulations are expected to be implemented on a timetable set by CARB as part of the regulatory process, which includes opportunities for public comment and review.
2ee4afacad834f859bc2:
Texas’ grid operator warned on Thursday that reliability risks are growing heading into summer, citing expected demand increases and constraints on the power system during peak heat.

In an updated reliability assessment, the Electric Reliability Council of Texas (ERCOT) said the state’s weather-driven electricity needs could stress generating resources and transmission capability during periods of extreme temperatures. ERCOT forecasts that demand will rise as more consumers and businesses add load, while the summer operating environment may be complicated by outages and limited generation availability in some areas.

The operator said it will continue to monitor weather, generator performance and equipment availability through the summer season, and it urged market participants to be prepared for tighter conditions. ERCOT also highlighted the importance of maintaining sufficient spinning and non-spinning reserves, which are used to balance the grid when sudden changes in generation or load occur.

ERCOT’s warning comes as the Texas system has faced recurring reliability challenges tied to heat waves, storms and the timing of maintenance outages. The operator said it uses a range of scenarios to evaluate whether supply and reserves will meet system needs, including cases that assume high demand and lower-than-ideal generation availability. ERCOT’s assessment focuses on both “firm” resources scheduled to run and the performance of generators under summer conditions.

ERCOT Chief Operating Officer Brad Jones said the agency’s planning process is designed to ensure the grid can withstand the kind of extreme conditions that drive peak usage. “Our goal is to provide visibility into potential risk areas well before the summer peak, so stakeholders can prepare and stay within reliability requirements,” Jones said in a statement.

The operator also noted that Texas has increasingly relied on a mix of solar and wind generation, which can vary by time of day and weather conditions. ERCOT said those fluctuations are accounted for in its assessments, but it remains critical for the system to have enough dispatchable generation and reserves to cover the gap when renewable output declines.

ERCOT said it expects to provide additional updates as summer approaches, including its next forecasts and operating guidance. The operator did not indicate that a shortage is inevitable, but said the risks associated with summer conditions warrant proactive preparation by generators, retail providers and grid planners.
6cbf114a7411a013b263:
New York’s planned changes to its environmental review process could slow offshore wind project approvals, according to industry groups and state officials, as developers adjust to a revised interpretation of the SEQRA permitting rules.

The State Environmental Quality Review Act (SEQRA) governs how agencies evaluate potential environmental impacts of large projects, including offshore wind farms, transmission lines and associated port upgrades. Under guidance released in recent months, New York agencies have signaled that certain offshore wind projects may face additional scrutiny at the state level, even when federal review has already occurred.

Developers say the modifications could extend timelines by requiring more detailed assessments of issues such as marine habitat impacts, construction-related noise and potential effects on commercial fisheries. They also argue that the revised approach may affect how projects are scoped and whether agencies must revisit analyses for changes made during the design process.

“Any added step in the SEQRA process can translate into months of delay, which is critical for offshore projects with tight engineering schedules and supply-chain commitments,” said a representative for an offshore wind developer, speaking on condition of anonymity because the group was not authorized to comment publicly.

State officials said the changes are intended to strengthen environmental review while maintaining coordination with federal permitting. An administration spokesperson said New York’s approach remains focused on complying with SEQRA requirements and ensuring that agencies have the information needed to consider potential impacts before issuing approvals.

The revised process comes as offshore wind remains central to New York’s efforts to meet clean energy targets and reduce greenhouse gas emissions. The state has also been developing offshore wind transmission capacity and upgrading facilities that support construction and maintenance, with several projects in different stages of review.

Industry stakeholders are seeking clarity on how the updated SEQRA guidance will apply to upcoming decisions, including whether agencies can rely on certain federal determinations or must undertake separate state-specific analyses. They say the uncertainty can lead to additional correspondence between agencies and applicants, and may require supplemental studies if regulators identify gaps.

Environmental advocates said a more rigorous review is necessary given the scale of offshore wind development, but they also pointed to the need for clear timelines to prevent projects from being caught in extended administrative procedures. New York’s review process is expected to be used for both new projects and amendments to existing permits as developers negotiate final project designs and mitigation plans.

For developers, the changes could affect investment planning for the next wave of offshore wind projects, with permitting timelines influencing contract schedules and equipment procurement. Several companies have not publicly confirmed whether they would pause new filings, but many are watching closely for updated guidance from state agencies on how SEQRA will be applied.
6acc1ed5f54ae4560f3b:
A gas transit deal between Russia and Ukraine expired on Sunday without a replacement agreement being reached, leaving uncertainty over future flows of Russian gas through Ukrainian pipelines to Europe.

The contract, which governed the booking and delivery of Russian gas volumes transiting Ukraine, ended at the close of the weekend. Negotiations on a successor arrangement had continued in recent days, but officials from both sides failed to finalize terms, according to industry participants familiar with the talks. The expiration comes amid ongoing efforts by European buyers to secure long-term supply routes following years of disruptions linked to the war.

With the deal lapse, shippers are expected to reassess nominations for transit capacity into Ukraine and reroute supplies where possible. Ukrainian operators, which manage the transit system, said earlier that they would continue to handle scheduled transit operations only under existing contractual frameworks. In the absence of a new accord, further transport of Russian gas through Ukrainian territory would depend on whether any limited, legally binding arrangements are made before the end of existing technical and commercial structures.

European energy markets have been watching the outcome closely because Ukraine has remained a key corridor for Russian gas deliveries to several countries in central and eastern Europe. The pipeline network allows gas to reach customers via connections into the European grid, though the volume and commercial terms have steadily shifted over time due to war-related restrictions, sanctions and competition from alternative supply.

In the near term, market impacts may depend on inventory levels, available liquefied natural gas (LNG) capacity and pipeline flows from other routes, including deliveries through the North and South corridors and direct imports via European LNG terminals. Several European utilities and traders have already increased purchases from non-Russian sources in recent months to reduce exposure to potential corridor failures.

A senior European industry source said the expiration “creates an immediate contractual gap” and that buyers would likely seek interim arrangements or rely on alternative supplies while negotiations continue. The source added that any new deal would need to address transit volumes, pricing and operational responsibility, including compliance with European regulations.

Russia and Ukraine have previously offered differing positions on the structure and duration of future transit. Further talks are expected, but no timeline for a replacement agreement was provided, leaving the next steps dependent on negotiations between governments and commercial operators on both sides.
6df5a8a097ba3ead0da2:
OPEC+ is considering additional oil production cuts as it weighs signs of a glut in global crude markets, according to sources familiar with the discussions ahead of upcoming policy talks.

The group, which includes members of OPEC and allies such as Russia, has been calibrating supply since oil prices weakened and commercial inventories rose in several regions. Officials have been assessing whether existing agreement volumes are sufficient to rebalance supply and demand, or whether further curbs are needed to tighten the market.

Several sources said deliberations are focused on how much production could be reduced and for how long, with attention on compliance and the impact of supply from non-OPEC countries. The discussions also consider current data on seasonal demand, refinery run rates and freight patterns, which influence expectations for short-term consumption.

OPEC+ has previously leaned on adjustments to reinforce discipline within the alliance, including mechanisms for countries to make up shortfalls when demand strengthens. Under the current framework, some participants can raise output levels gradually in line with agreed schedules, while others face limits linked to their performance and baseline production. Market watchers have said the credibility of those schedules is central to OPEC+ decisions.

One official involved in the negotiations said the group is working from “a full picture of inventories and forward indicators” to decide next steps, adding that there is “no automatic choice” to extend or deepen cuts without clear evidence that the oversupply persists. The same official noted that the group will also factor in the pace of compliance by member states and any unexpected changes in non-OPEC output.

The consultations come as Brent and West Texas Intermediate futures have fluctuated around levels that reflect uncertainty over the balance of supply. Traders have pointed to continued growth in U.S. shale volumes, steady output from parts of Latin America and the possibility of delays or accelerations in new project ramp-ups.

If OPEC+ agrees on additional reductions, the decision could be framed as either an extension of existing quotas or a new phase of cuts, depending on how officials judge inventory levels later in the first half. A final determination is expected to follow further internal meetings, with markets likely to react to any signals from delegates during the run-up to the next scheduled gathering.
7fc70423560b0eae4274:
China approved a record number of new coal-fired power plants last year, reaching the highest level in a decade, according to data compiled by environmental groups that track the permitting process.

The approvals mark a sharp departure from the pace of recent years as policymakers have pledged to curb coal consumption and tighten power-sector emissions. Nevertheless, the record suggests demand for electricity growth and efforts to maintain grid reliability are still driving new capacity decisions, the groups said.

Across China, provincial authorities approved coal projects that would add substantial generation capacity, with several approvals concentrated in inland regions where demand has been rising faster and where existing power plants are increasingly constrained. The approvals include both new plants and units tied to broader expansions at existing facilities.

Environmental advocates said the permitting surge raises the risk of long-lived assets being built ahead of a shift toward cleaner energy sources. Coal plants can operate for decades, making the upfront approvals a key indicator of the power sector’s trajectory for years to come.

A spokesperson for one of the groups tracking the approvals said the permitting pipeline is “picking up again” after earlier slowdowns, pointing to a higher share of projects moving through the approval stage. The spokesperson added that the concentration of approvals in particular provinces suggests uneven enforcement of national guidance on capacity additions.

Chinese officials have repeatedly said the country will peak carbon dioxide emissions by 2030 and achieve carbon neutrality by 2060, while also ensuring energy security. In recent years, Beijing has promoted renewables, accelerated grid expansion and introduced policies intended to limit new coal capacity. Those measures have included stricter approvals for some regions and greater use of ultra-supercritical technology, as well as requirements tied to efficiency and emissions controls.

The approval figures underscore the challenge of balancing emissions targets with the need for stable power supply during periods of rapid economic growth and uneven renewable output. Analysts say electricity demand is influenced by industrial production, weather and the pace of electrification, which can change the balance between coal and alternatives.

China’s national and provincial permitting authorities typically assess local grid needs, fuel supply conditions and environmental reviews before approving coal projects. The latest decade-high level of approvals suggests those assessments have continued to favor additional coal capacity despite mounting pressure from climate goals.
532de4a27923a96f871a:
Australia on Wednesday announced a new critical-minerals export strategy aimed at expanding supplies of minerals used in batteries, clean energy technologies and defence systems, as governments worldwide seek more reliable resource chains.

The strategy sets out priorities for Australia’s government agencies and industry to identify export opportunities, streamline approval processes and strengthen downstream processing, according to a statement from the Department of Industry, Science and Resources. It focuses on minerals including lithium, nickel, cobalt, rare earths, graphite and manganese, as well as potential emerging feedstocks.

Under the plan, the government will work with states and territories to improve access to transport infrastructure and reduce bottlenecks that can delay shipments, particularly where projects face land use, environmental and permitting constraints. It also proposes new measures to support exploration and development, including funding for geological surveys and support for early-stage projects through targeted programs.

A key element is an effort to diversify export markets and deepen trading links with countries that are building or expanding manufacturing capacity for electric vehicles, wind turbines and energy storage. The government said it will also seek new bilateral arrangements to promote long-term offtake deals and reduce price and supply volatility for buyers.

The strategy includes provisions to improve data transparency around critical-minerals production and supply, with the aim of helping industry and trading partners plan procurement. It also commits to monitoring environmental and social performance, including through stronger reporting requirements for projects receiving government support.

“The world is looking for secure supplies of critical minerals and Australia is responding with a strategy that supports investment, improves certainty for industry and helps ensure responsible development,” Resources Minister Madeleine King said in the statement.

Australia is one of the world’s major suppliers of several critical minerals. It is a leading exporter of lithium and is also significant in rare earth separation capacity in parts of its supply chain, though most processing remains concentrated in limited countries. The government said the export strategy is intended to build on existing strengths while positioning Australia for growing demand from manufacturing hubs.

The announcement comes as countries and companies accelerate efforts to build “secure and sustainable” mineral supply chains, frequently citing concerns about reliance on single producers, geopolitical risk and environmental impacts. Australia said it would publish progress updates and consult with industry, regulators and affected communities as projects move through development and export approvals.
28b93795565fbdf14f74:
India’s latest solar auction drew record-low bids, according to government data released on Thursday, reflecting continued pressure on prices as developers compete for contracts under the country’s expanding renewable energy programme.

The auction, part of the Solar Energy Corporation of India (SECI) tendering framework, attracted bids at the lowest levels seen in recent rounds, with winning tariffs falling below prior auction benchmarks for both utility-scale and rooftop-linked projects. The results underscore how increased supply capacity, improved panel manufacturing and tighter cost assumptions have driven developers to quote more aggressively for future generation sales.

SECI said it received bids from a range of domestic and international developers, with participation remaining broad even as tariffs continued to edge down. Several projects were awarded contracts after meeting technical and qualification requirements, and the selected developers will have to meet commissioning deadlines set out in the bid documents. The contracts, typically structured around long-term power purchase agreements, require delivery of power to distribution companies at the quoted tariff.

The record-low outcomes come as India continues to push toward higher renewable generation targets under its national energy transition plans. Solar has been a central pillar of that strategy, with authorities seeking to add gigawatts of capacity annually while keeping electricity prices competitive. In recent years, repeated auctions have gradually reduced tariffs, although price movements have varied across tranches depending on location, land and transmission conditions, and the contract structure.

Industry analysts said lower bids are also consistent with expectations of continued declines in key input costs, including modules and inverters, as well as lower financing costs for projects that secure credit support and bankable offtake arrangements. They also noted that developers may be factoring in supply chain efficiencies and learning effects from earlier procurement rounds.

SECI did not provide a detailed breakdown of each component of the bid pricing in the announcement, but one senior official said the auction results reflect “strong competition among bidders and improved project readiness across the pipeline.” The agency said it will proceed with the next phases of contract signing and monitoring to ensure timely execution.

The auction adds to a series of recent tenders in which solar tariffs have fallen to multi-year lows, including separate rounds for renewable energy zones and competitive bids for storage-linked solar. Developers awarded capacity are expected to begin construction soon, subject to land availability, grid connectivity and regulatory approvals.

With the latest auction results, policymakers are likely to use the pricing outcome as a reference point for future tender designs, including risk allocation, off-taker terms and timing of payments, as India balances rapid deployment with cost control.
77978bfa7939b7b4e1fc:
The African Development Bank has expanded a fund dedicated to financing grid infrastructure projects to $3 billion, aiming to speed up the rollout of electricity transmission and distribution across the continent, the lender said.

The bank said the enhanced facility will support investment in regional interconnections and national grid reinforcement, including substations, transmission lines and last‑mile distribution networks. It is designed to mobilize additional public and private capital by providing financing in areas where utilities and developers often face long development timelines and high upfront costs.

The expansion follows the bank’s assessment that demand for electricity is rising while many countries continue to grapple with underpowered grids, limited cross‑border connections and persistent reliability issues. The AfDB said improving grid capacity and reliability is a prerequisite for broader power generation expansion, including renewables, and for expanding access in underserved regions.

Under the updated structure, the fund will continue to invest through a mix of instruments, including loans, guarantees and risk‑sharing measures intended to reduce financing costs and de‑risk projects for commercial lenders. The bank said it will prioritize projects that strengthen regional power trade and address bottlenecks that constrain generation dispatch, as well as those linked to electrification plans and better grid governance.

AfDB President Akinwumi Adesina said the scale-up is intended to make capital available earlier in the project pipeline and to widen the range of grid assets that can be funded. “Electricity grids are the backbone of the power sector, and scaling up this facility will help ensure that the investments needed to connect communities and integrate clean energy are not delayed by financing gaps,” he said in a statement.

The bank did not provide a country-by-country list of projects at this stage, but said the first set of investments will be selected based on readiness, developmental impact and the potential to leverage co‑financing. It also indicated that the fund will work with utilities, regulators and regional power pools to support technical studies, development planning and procurement processes that can determine whether projects reach financial close.

The $3 billion target makes the facility one of the larger dedicated grid finance programs under the AfDB’s broader power and infrastructure agenda. The bank said it expects the expanded fund to improve project pipeline visibility and accelerate disbursements over the coming years as additional sponsors move toward construction and operations.
541ccf7c2bc37f1e08d6:
The United Nations’ Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) has published a draft synthesis report for its Working Group III, which focuses on climate change mitigation, ahead of a wider assessment due later this year.

The draft synthesis document consolidates findings from the IPCC’s Working Group III assessments on limiting greenhouse gas emissions, covering sectors such as energy supply, industry, buildings, transport and land-based mitigation. It also reviews options for reducing emissions while addressing issues including sustainable development, energy security and affordability.

The IPCC said the working group’s draft is intended to support governments and experts in preparing the final version of the report, which will draw on the underlying technical reports and special assessments released in recent months. The synthesis format is designed to provide an integrated overview of the evidence base, update key conclusions and present a cross-cutting assessment of pathways for emissions reductions across different contexts.

According to the IPCC, the draft reflects the latest available literature and incorporates comments received during earlier rounds of review. It is expected to undergo further consultation with governments and experts before its final publication, with the process including the resolution of review comments to ensure consistency and accuracy across sections.

The Working Group III assessment also addresses the feasibility and impacts of mitigation measures, including differences in costs and implementation challenges across countries and regions. It covers the role of policy, technology and behavior-related changes, as well as the importance of planning and investment decisions that affect emissions trajectories over coming decades.

In addition, the report draft discusses the relationship between mitigation efforts and other societal goals, including adaptation co-benefits and the reduction of air pollution. It examines the impacts of transitioning energy systems and the potential for carbon management strategies, including reducing methane emissions and improving efficiency.

The IPCC’s synthesis process is part of the panel’s broader effort to produce an up-to-date, consensus-based assessment for policymakers. The Working Group III draft will be followed by additional steps in the review cycle, leading to the final synthesis report and its summary for policymakers.
ccb8841677ace72fcc66:
A new study published in *Nature Climate* reports that the Amazon rainforest is absorbing carbon more slowly than previously estimated, signaling weaker momentum in the region’s long-running role as a carbon sink.

Using satellite-based observations and updated calculations of how vegetation responds to changes in climate and land use, the researchers found that the Amazon’s rate of carbon uptake has declined compared with earlier assessments. The study attributes part of the slowdown to recent years of drought stress and heat, along with ongoing forest degradation linked to fire and land clearing. The authors say the revised numbers do not indicate a sudden reversal of the Amazon’s carbon balance, but they do point to reduced capacity to offset emissions.

The findings also highlight differences across the basin. Some regions with more stable rainfall and intact forest cover continued to capture carbon at relatively higher rates, while areas affected by repeated dry seasons and disturbance showed sharper declines. The paper describes the Amazon’s response as increasingly variable, with wetter periods failing to fully compensate for losses during harsher conditions.

Scientists said the new work helps refine estimates that have been central to climate projections and carbon accounting. Carbon uptake determines how much atmospheric carbon is removed by growing vegetation, and changes in that process can alter the trajectory of global warming. Accurate measurements are also used in national and international efforts to monitor forest-related emissions, including commitments tied to deforestation reduction.

“By combining observational data with improved methods for estimating carbon uptake, we can better quantify how climate-driven stress and land-use impacts are changing the Amazon’s function as a sink,” one of the study’s authors said. The researchers added that the slowdown is consistent with a broader pattern of weakened ecosystem resilience in tropical forests under increasing climate extremes.

The study comes as governments and companies increasingly rely on near-real-time monitoring to assess deforestation and forest health. While the Amazon remains a critical buffer in the global carbon cycle, the authors say the declining uptake rate raises the stakes for conservation and restoration efforts. They call for continued observation and for models to incorporate the latest satellite and field measurements to track whether the trend persists in coming years.
81a6b073b6dc0b19c3a3:
Glaciologists have reported record-low Arctic sea ice levels for March, with extent and coverage falling to the lowest values observed in recent satellite records, according to new analyses.

Researchers said the decline reflects a combination of reduced ice formation in late winter and unusually strong melting at the end of the previous season, which can leave more thin, easily eroded ice entering the spring. They added that sea ice that survives the summer is generally more resilient, but this year’s ice has been increasingly vulnerable to weather-driven heat and wind patterns.

The study, released this week by an international team of cryosphere specialists, found that March sea ice extent dropped below prior records for the month, continuing a trend of weaker ice conditions across the Arctic. Sea ice extent refers to the area covered by at least 15% ice concentration, a common benchmark used to track year-to-year changes. The team also reported that ice concentration in many regions was lower than normal, indicating that not only the edges of the ice pack retreated, but the central ice itself was less dense.

While the report focused on March conditions, the researchers said the situation is rooted in earlier seasonal changes. Warmer-than-average air temperatures over parts of the Arctic can accelerate surface melting and delay freeze-up, while ocean heat can suppress ice growth from below. Wind-driven movement of ice can further concentrate openings of open water and spread thinner ice across wider areas, affecting both short-term thickness and longer-term survival.

“March is typically when the ice reaches a relatively stable seasonal maximum, so a record low at this time is a strong signal of ongoing change,” said lead researcher Dr. Lena Fischer. She added that the findings align with expectations from long-term warming trends and highlight the importance of monitoring how winter conditions set up the following summer.

Scientists said the new record comes as routine satellite monitoring continues through the spring melt season. They cautioned that year-to-year variability can be influenced by atmospheric circulation and storms, but said the overall magnitude of the decline points to a persistent reduction in Arctic sea-ice resilience. Continued observations, including assessments of thickness and ice drift, are expected to refine projections for the remainder of the melt period and the upcoming autumn freeze-up.
c416e8081cc2c8ad79f4:
The UK government has launched a consultation on the design of contracts-for-difference (CfD) “round 7,” the latest step in its process to bring forward long-term support for new low-carbon power generation, according to documents published on Tuesday.

The consultation sets out proposed parameters for the next allocation scheme under the CfD regime, which provides developers with payments designed to stabilise revenue for electricity generated from eligible technologies. The government said the aim is to create a framework that is investable, delivers value for consumers and can support delivery of renewable and other low-carbon projects.

A key part of the consultation focuses on how CfDs would be structured for different technologies and delivery timelines. The government said it is seeking views on the level of administrative strike prices, eligibility and auction design elements that determine how bids are assessed and contracts are awarded. The proposals also look at how performance and delivery risks are handled, including requirements intended to ensure projects reach construction and commissioning milestones.

The consultation comes as the UK tries to sustain investment in electricity generation while balancing cost controls and grid readiness. CfD rounds have historically supported offshore wind, onshore wind in limited circumstances, solar and other low-carbon generation, with specific eligibility and contract terms changing over time.

The government is also consulting on wider procedural aspects, including how the allocation process would operate, how participants would demonstrate compliance with scheme requirements and how the government would manage the transition between rounds. It said responses would inform the final design of the auction rules and the draft allocation framework that will be published closer to the start of the next procurement cycle.

“CfD is central to enabling investment in low-carbon electricity, and we want round 7 to build on lessons learned from previous rounds to make sure contracts are competitive and secure,” a spokesperson for the Department for Energy Security and Net Zero said in a statement.

The consultation period is expected to run for several weeks, with the government planning to publish a response document summarising changes before the CfD allocation framework is finalised. Details of the likely competition scope, including which technologies may participate and any capacity or budget constraints, are expected to be confirmed as the process moves forward.
04fe1e688d5ada9751f3:
Norway’s sovereign wealth fund, the world’s largest, has divested from its remaining holdings of thermal coal as it continues tightening environmental exclusions across its portfolio.

The decision, announced on Wednesday by Norges Bank Investment Management, affects companies the fund still held with significant exposure to thermal coal, which is used to generate electricity. The firm said it would sell the relevant equity holdings and also reduce investments linked to coal-fired power generation in line with the fund’s coal policy, which has been progressively strengthened in recent years.

Under the updated approach, companies that derive a specified share of revenue from thermal coal activities or that are primarily engaged in coal extraction and related power generation are no longer eligible. Norges Bank Investment Management said it would implement the divestment in accordance with its established rules, including planned timelines that allow for orderly selling and the avoidance of market disruption.

The fund has been narrowing its exposure to fossil-fuel segments for more than a decade, using an exclusion framework that is separate from its voting and engagement activities. In earlier steps, it moved away from companies producing coal for power generation as well as firms that fail to meet environmental and emissions standards.

Norges Bank Investment Management said its coal policy is intended to reflect updated assessments of climate risk and the development of international standards. The firm also reiterated that it continues to monitor companies’ activities to determine whether they meet exclusion criteria, and whether any companies could later qualify for potential re-inclusion if they materially change their business.

The Norwegian government’s fund holds assets worth more than $1 trillion, invested globally across equities, fixed income and real estate. Divestment decisions can have ripple effects beyond Norway because the fund is widely followed by other asset managers and companies, and because it invests through large, liquid market holdings.

A spokesperson for Norges Bank Investment Management said the latest move “ensures the fund remains aligned with its coal strategy and gradually reduces exposure to companies involved in thermal coal,” while maintaining its broader investment diversification.
01631ec2ceb8345be3e0:
Chile has proposed a framework for pricing exports of green hydrogen, aiming to provide clearer rules for contracts and reduce uncertainty for developers and buyers, the government said on Thursday.

The proposal, prepared by Chile’s Ministry of Energy, sets out an approach to determine reference prices for green hydrogen produced using renewable power. It is intended to support future export negotiations and align domestic regulation with international demand, particularly for long-term offtake agreements in Asia and Europe.

Under the framework, prices would be linked to the cost structure of producing green hydrogen in Chile, including renewable electricity generation, water inputs and electrolyser-related costs. The ministry said the methodology would take into account project-specific variables such as capacity, electricity supply profiles and storage or transport requirements, while also defining how to handle currency conversion and payment terms. It would also specify eligibility criteria for projects that qualify as producing “green” hydrogen under Chile’s existing standards.

The government said it is designing the mechanism to be compatible with both spot and contract-based sales, though it places emphasis on long-term arrangements commonly used for large-scale export infrastructure such as pipelines, shipping logistics and port facilities.

Chile has positioned itself as a major future supplier of green hydrogen and its derivatives, including ammonia and synthetic fuels, given its large renewable resource base and plans for industrial-scale production in the north. Several international companies have announced projects in the country, but pricing terms have remained a central issue for investors, given high upfront costs and the need to ensure bankable returns.

“The goal is to establish a transparent reference for pricing green hydrogen exports that can be used in negotiations, while reflecting the real production conditions in Chile,” the ministry said in a statement. It added that the framework is intended to facilitate financing by providing a consistent basis for assessing revenue potential and risk.

Chile said the proposal will now be open for consultation with industry stakeholders, including developers, financiers and potential importers. The government plans to refine the model based on feedback and further technical work before moving toward implementation measures, officials said.
cfc9be94a4dd11b009c7:
South Korea on Saturday unveiled a new plan to expand its nuclear energy buildout, aiming to add additional reactor capacity while tightening oversight of fuel cycles and safety management.

The government said the roadmap, released by the Ministry of Trade, Industry and Energy, sets out a framework for building new reactors and extending the operating lives of existing plants, alongside measures to strengthen regulatory capacity and emergency preparedness. The ministry said the plan is intended to help stabilize electricity supply and reduce reliance on imported fuels as demand grows.

Under the proposal, South Korea targets additional nuclear capacity through a mix of new builds and life extensions, with the exact project sequence dependent on licensing timelines and local approval processes. The ministry said it will update siting and design standards to reflect lessons learned from recent reactor construction and performance reviews, including requirements for further redundancy in safety systems.

The plan also lays out steps for the nuclear fuel cycle, including expanded use of domestic enrichment capabilities and continued work on spent fuel management. South Korea has been pursuing reprocessing and recycling technologies, while maintaining safeguards and reporting requirements under international agreements. The government said it will continue coordinating with the International Atomic Energy Agency and relevant partners to ensure compliance with non-proliferation commitments.

A key element of the roadmap is a stronger approach to nuclear workforce development, the ministry said, including training programs for reactor operators, maintenance personnel and engineering staff. It added that it will work to increase capacity for nuclear plant safety inspections and technical assessments, including through upgrades to information systems used by regulators.

“We are presenting a concrete path to expand nuclear energy in a way that is safe, predictable for industry and transparent to the public,” a ministry official said, adding that the timetable will be adjusted based on project readiness and regulatory review outcomes.

The announcement comes as South Korea, which currently operates a fleet of domestically built nuclear reactors, weighs long-term energy policy against public concerns about safety and waste disposal. The government said it will conduct consultations with local communities and legislative bodies as specific sites are advanced.

Energy analysts said the plan’s impact will depend on how quickly new reactor licensing and construction can be completed, as well as on procurement of critical components. The ministry said further details on reactor types, schedules and budget allocations will be released in subsequent phases.
8e31cb91f8b276d5dbca:
Japanese trading houses are committing larger stakes to long-term LNG offtake deals, signaling a renewed strategy to secure supplies amid volatility in gas markets and rising competition for tanker and pipeline capacity, according to industry and company sources.

Executives at several firms said the shift reflects both commercial pricing opportunities and the need to ensure reliable volumes for utilities and industrial customers as countries tighten gas import planning for the second half of the decade. Long-term arrangements, typically structured with take-or-pay clauses and multi-year delivery schedules, can help reduce exposure to spot-market swings, they said.

Mitsubishi Corp, trading under a wide portfolio of energy contracts, has been negotiating additional LNG purchase arrangements linked to incremental cargoes and new terminals, people familiar with the discussions said. Marubeni and Itochu have also explored expansions of existing supply frameworks, including options for flexibility around seasonal demand, the sources added. While the terms vary by counterpart and destination, several deals are understood to be built around multi-year volumes that can be rerouted or adjusted under predefined conditions.

Industry participants said the move comes as Japan—one of the world’s largest LNG buyers—continues to rebalance procurement after years of rapid demand growth and supply diversification. Several utilities have been reviewing hedging and contracting approaches, weighing long-run supply costs against the risk of over-contracted volumes if demand softens due to efficiency gains or slower industrial activity.

“We are looking to secure the right volumes over the long term while maintaining commercial flexibility as the market evolves,” one senior executive at a major Japanese trading house said in an interview, without naming specific contracts.

The strategy also intersects with broader constraints facing LNG supply chains, including limited ability to lock in shipping slots and commissioning timelines for new export projects. With competition increasing among Asian buyers, suppliers are seeking customers willing to provide stable offtake commitments tied to specific train outputs or delivery windows, sources said.

Trading houses, which often act as intermediaries between producers and end-users, are positioning themselves as sponsors for financing and cargo routing arrangements. They may also play a role in bridging infrastructure gaps, such as where regasification capacity is available but contracting terms or tanker scheduling require coordination across multiple counterparties.

Details of the latest negotiations were not immediately available, and several firms declined to comment. However, analysts said the uptick in long-duration contracting aligns with how Japanese firms have historically managed energy security by building layered procurement portfolios that blend long-term stability with selective spot sourcing.
1dc62eaf0be5da2f75ca:
The U.S. Department of Energy on Monday launched a new $2 billion grant program aimed at strengthening the resilience of the nation’s electric grid against extreme weather, cyber threats and other disruptions.

DOE said the funding will support projects designed to help utilities and grid operators better withstand and recover from outages, including investments in advanced grid monitoring, hardened infrastructure, improved protection systems and planning activities. The program is intended to improve the reliability of electricity delivery in communities that have experienced repeated disruptions, DOE officials said.

The department said grants under the initiative will be available to a range of entities, including state and local governments, utilities, grid operators and nonprofit organizations. DOE did not specify the number of awards expected, but said it plans to distribute funds through competitive solicitations that will evaluate project readiness, the scale of expected benefits and alignment with resilience goals in regional grid plans.

DOE also linked the program to its broader efforts to modernize the electricity system, noting that upgrades could include backup power for critical facilities, enhanced transmission and distribution resilience, and upgrades that allow parts of the grid to isolate faults and restore service faster. The department said applicants will be expected to describe how projects reduce risk and improve recovery timelines, including how they will measure performance after funding ends.

“Resilient power systems are essential to keeping communities safe, supporting critical services and reducing the impact of disruptions,” an administration official said in a statement. The official said DOE will prioritize projects that demonstrate clear, measurable outcomes and that help strengthen grid performance during emergencies.

The announcement comes as utilities across the country seek to address growing stress on infrastructure, including heat waves, hurricanes, wildfires and flooding that can damage transmission lines and substations. The program also reflects increased focus on cybersecurity and coordination among grid stakeholders, with DOE saying applicants should consider how systems and operating procedures can improve response during both physical and digital incidents.

DOE said additional details about application requirements, deadlines and evaluation criteria will be published as part of upcoming funding opportunities. Grant guidance is expected to be released in phases, with DOE indicating that the program will build toward larger resilience improvements while supporting near-term upgrades.
3ad758b5a50ca3cdc930:
The U.S. Environmental Protection Agency on Tuesday finalized greenhouse gas standards for power plants, setting out new limits and compliance timelines for fossil-fuel generation under the agency’s Clean Air Act authority.

The final rule, issued after months of proposal and public comment, establishes performance requirements for carbon dioxide emissions from existing power plants and sets specific requirements for new or modified units. The EPA said the measure is designed to reduce emissions while accounting for operational and reliability needs in different regions of the country.

Under the framework, states will be required to translate the federal emissions targets into enforceable plans. Those plans, which will be submitted to the EPA for approval, are expected to include a mix of approaches such as emissions-rate improvements, coal retirements where applicable, and generation shifts toward lower-emitting resources. The rule also includes monitoring, reporting and verification requirements for power plant emissions data.

EPA officials said the regulation was finalized after review of the agency’s legal authority and technical assumptions used to calculate emission reductions. In a statement, EPA Administrator said the final standards provide “clear, enforceable expectations” for industry and states and support continued progress in cutting pollution from the power sector.

The EPA’s final action also addresses how compliance would work for different types of generating units and how the agency will handle changes to the power system over time. The rule specifies that emissions reductions will be measured using facility-specific and aggregated metrics, and it includes provisions intended to prevent backsliding when units are retired or repowered.

Industry and environmental groups are likely to assess the rule’s effect on operating costs, investment timelines and the pace of coal generation declines. Several states that have previously supported federal power-plant emission limits are expected to begin the planning process immediately, while other states may evaluate whether to challenge the rule in court, citing questions about federal authority and grid impacts.

The EPA said it will continue to oversee state plan development and enforcement, including reviewing submissions and final approvals for compliance steps. With the final rule in place, states will face new deadlines for building and submitting implementation plans as the agency moves toward nationwide rollout of the standards.
7d8c67952a5aa0080855:
Climate scientists on Thursday urged governments to update policies and emissions pledges to keep the world on track for limiting warming to 1.5 degrees Celsius, warning that current national plans are still not aligned with the goal.

In a call published ahead of key international climate discussions, researchers said countries should revise near-term targets, tighten implementation of existing measures and improve the transparency of progress. They cited continuing gaps between projected emissions reductions under current commitments and the cuts required to slow warming within the 1.5C threshold.

The scientists said the latest global emissions trajectory would need stronger action across energy, industry, transport and buildings, along with accelerated deployment of clean power and efficiency measures. They also pointed to the need for clearer planning around methane abatement, land-use changes and resilience efforts, noting that non-carbon dioxide gases and ecosystem impacts affect the pace and pattern of warming.

A central focus of the update request is timing. Researchers said the next few years are critical to avoid “locking in” higher emissions levels through infrastructure investments in power generation, vehicles and heat supply. They added that many countries are relying on long-term net-zero pledges that lack detailed near-term steps, making it harder to track whether pathways are being adjusted in time.

“Keeping 1.5C within reach requires immediate recalibration of national plans, not just long-term commitments,” one author said in the statement. “Governments need to show how policies will deliver emissions reductions in the 2026–2030 window and report progress with consistent, verifiable data.”

The call also highlighted the role of finance and technology transfer in enabling faster cuts, especially in emerging economies. Scientists said adaptation planning should run alongside emissions reductions, but warned that delaying mitigation increases the scale of future climate impacts and costs.

The update request comes as countries prepare for climate negotiations and reviews of their commitments under the Paris Agreement. Under the agreement’s framework, governments submit updated national plans and track progress over time, with scientific assessments used to evaluate whether collective action is sufficient.

Researchers said they expect governments to consider additional policies that could close gaps, including faster permitting for renewable energy, expanded grid capacity, tighter building standards, and strengthened controls on industrial and waste emissions. They urged greater consistency between national targets and accounting methods, to support comparability across countries and strengthen accountability.
5ad2b0b564cd02d5f7ff:
Saudi Arabian oil giant Saudi Aramco said it is expanding its petrochemicals investment in Asia, stepping up plans to supply plastics and chemical feedstocks to growing regional demand.

The company outlined the move in an investor update, describing new opportunities across its downstream portfolio as it seeks to grow earnings beyond crude oil. Aramco said it would prioritize projects that strengthen its chemical value chain and expand access to export markets, with a focus on countries where demand for polymers, specialty chemicals and industrial intermediates is rising.

Aramco did not provide the full list of projects in the update, but indicated that the investment push would build on existing partnerships and infrastructure in Asia. It said it is working with industrial and government stakeholders to evaluate new capacity and supply arrangements, including potential expansions at existing facilities and new derivative plants tied to existing supply hubs.

Petrochemicals have become a larger part of Aramco’s downstream growth strategy as the company seeks to convert crude and gas into higher-value products. The update said the expansion is aligned with Aramco’s longer-term plan to increase production in chemicals and to broaden its customer base, while maintaining investment discipline and market-led development.

The company said it expects the Asia expansion to complement its global refining and chemicals network, which includes investments and joint ventures in multiple regions. It also referenced ongoing work to improve operational efficiency and reliability across its downstream assets, including maintenance programs and optimization of product slates to match changing demand patterns.

Aramco Chief Executive Amin Nasser said in the update that the company sees strong opportunities to grow its chemicals business in Asia as industrial consumption expands. “We are focusing on projects with clear commercial logic and the ability to meet evolving customer needs,” Nasser said.

Aramco’s announcement comes as global chemical markets grapple with shifting trade flows, capacity expansions in parts of Asia, and competition among major producers to secure feedstock supply. It also reflects the broader trend of Gulf energy groups increasing downstream exposure to capture more value across refining and chemical products.

The company said timing and final approvals for new projects will depend on regulatory processes, partner agreements, and market conditions, without specifying a target timeline or investment amount for the Asia expansion.
2a2b1e15b449bcd5ae4e:
Global prices for key battery metals rebounded this month after an 18-month slump as investors bet demand will recover and supply disruptions tightened the market balance, according to data from major trading and pricing services.

Lithium, nickel and cobalt all rose from multi-month lows, with gains concentrated in spot and near-term contracts. The improvement followed a period in which prices had been weighed down by slowing electric-vehicle sales growth in several regions, oversupply from new production and inventory build-ups among converters and refiners.

In the lithium market, benchmark prices increased across several grade categories, helped by firmer demand from battery manufacturers and expectations of reduced availability of spodumene feedstock in the second half. Traders said spot premiums were more readily quoted as buyers returned to the market, narrowing the gap between spot and contracted prices. Some sellers have also been more selective with shipments amid higher freight costs and varying quality specifications from new sources.

Nickel prices rebounded as well, supported by tighter inventories and short-covering after a prolonged downturn. While demand signals remain uneven, particularly for stainless steel and industrial uses, supply constraints in certain refining routes have limited the near-term availability of refined material. Market participants also pointed to improved liquidity in exchanges and fewer arbitrage opportunities that had kept prices suppressed.

Cobalt rose from depressed levels as the market adjusted to supply dynamics in the Democratic Republic of the Congo and related refining flows. Buyers cited improved visibility on contracted offtake schedules, while refiners continued to manage production rates to align with demand.
