451a4b9b57e7b1e951d8:
Rare-earth buyers reported thin order books ahead of the May holiday period, signaling limited trading activity in several key product grades and a cautious approach to restocking for the early summer demand window.

Dealers said quotes for neodymium-praseodymium (NdPr), praseodymium (Pr) and other separated rare-earth materials were available but that confirmed volumes remained low. Several market participants attributed the thinness to calendar effects, with suppliers and buyers reducing the number of purchase orders placed during the lead-up to the holiday, and some contracts being pushed into the next operating cycle.

In the NdPr segment, sources described a market where offers were present but where buyers sought clearer guidance on pricing and availability before committing larger tonnages. One trader said that while “the market is not closed,” participants have been “waiting for clearer visibility” on production schedules and on how the next wave of demand will be reflected in pricing.

Metal and oxide markets also showed signs of muted interest, with buyers focusing on smaller quantities to cover immediate needs rather than building inventory. Market watchers said that, in past years, holiday-adjacent gaps in shipping and processing can temporarily reduce the number of deliveries and therefore suppress order book depth.

Several factors have continued to shape the overall supply outlook, including upstream rare-earth concentrate sourcing, the stability of separation capacity, and the timing of shipments tied to downstream manufacturing cycles. Traders also pointed to the pace of restocking by rare-earth permanent magnet producers, which can influence demand for NdPr and related products.

Despite the low order book activity, participants said they expected trading to pick up after the holiday period as buyers return to normal procurement routines. Suppliers, meanwhile, were largely monitoring whether any changes in logistics or production plans would affect lead times and available volumes, with most attention shifting to the next few weeks for confirmation of demand and pricing momentum.
a5d395c6ab0dd263b0d0: Rare-earth inventory levels in Shanghai have largely normalised after a brief tightening in late January, according to market participants tracking spot availability. By 2026-02-25, mixed rare-earth supplies at major downstream hubs were reported within 5% to 10% of baseline stock, following days when order fulfilment times extended to 10–14 working days. The improvement coincided with steadier deliveries of separated neodymium-praseodymium and dysprosium-bearing grades from contracted domestic producers, with transport volumes returning to near-typical weekly cadence of about 1,200–1,600 tonnes. Spot premiums that had widened in early February eased, and sellers reduced offerings of low-grade material as demand stabilised. Traders said inventory visibility improved after wholesalers rebalanced stock across bonded and domestic warehouses, cutting overstocks in faster-moving categories while holding back slower-moving compounds. Prices were described as stable into the 2026-03-01 week, with most movements limited to single-digit percentage changes as buyers moved from emergency replenishment to scheduled procurement.

Rare-earth magnet producers are signaling flat sales volumes for the second quarter, according to industry guidance.  
Demand from electric motor and industrial drive customers is expected to hold steady, with no broad rebound in orders.

fca63180e4eb00e37467:
Lynas Rare Earths has begun work to expand its Lynas Kalgoorlie operations in Western Australia, in a move aimed at increasing capacity for the processing of rare-earth concentrate and supporting demand from magnets and other clean-energy supply chains.

The expansion, described by the company as part of a multi-stage growth plan, is focused on upgrading processing units and associated infrastructure at the Kalgoorlie site, which is located in the Goldfields region and forms part of Lynas’s downstream pathway from mined ore to separated rare-earth products. The Kalgoorlie facility is primarily geared toward processing rare-earth feed to produce intermediate and mixed rare-earth products that are then further refined at other stages of the company’s operations.

Under the project scope, Lynas is adding equipment intended to lift throughput and improve operational flexibility, while also undertaking modifications to support increased volumes and maintain product specifications. The company said the upgrades include work to streamline material handling and enhance process control systems. It also said the project is being implemented in a way designed to keep existing production running where possible, with staged commissioning of new assets.

Lynas has faced scrutiny in recent years over the permitting and environmental management of its rare-earth processing activities, particularly given the regulatory sensitivity of rare-earth residue and chemical handling. In Kalgoorlie, operations are subject to state and local approvals and a range of environmental controls covering air emissions, water use and waste management. The expansion is being carried out alongside ongoing compliance activities, the company said.

A Lynas spokesperson said the Kalgoorlie expansion is designed to “strengthen our ability to convert concentrate into rare-earth products at higher rates,” while maintaining quality and safety requirements. The spokesperson added that Lynas is working through procurement and engineering milestones and expects the expanded configuration to deliver additional capacity progressively after commissioning.

Rare earths are used in high-performance magnets for wind turbines, electric vehicles and generators, as well as in defense, electronics and industrial applications. Demand forecasts have intensified efforts across the industry to secure diversified processing capacity outside China, where much of the global refining is concentrated.

The Kalgoorlie expansion comes as companies and governments seek more reliable supply of separated rare-earth materials, with downstream manufacturers increasingly looking for producers that can demonstrate traceability and consistent outputs. For Lynas, the project is one element in building a longer-term supply network from mining through to separation and product sales.
ade4fb2deb7d55b1e0b9: A new paper in Nature reports lab-scale progress in recycling rare-earth magnets using a process that targets neodymium and dysprosium recovery from waste materials. The study, published in the March 2026 issue, describes a multistep workflow that combines controlled acid leaching, solvent extraction and selective precipitation, with yields measured on gram-scale feedstock. According to the paper, researchers achieved recovery rates of about 70–85% for targeted rare-earth oxides under optimized chemistry and temperature windows, including conditions held at roughly 60–90°C and reaction times of several hours. The authors report that impurities such as iron, calcium and aluminum were reduced through staged purification, improving the final product purity to levels suitable for reuse in magnet-grade precursors. Bench tests also showed that reagent consumption could be kept within a low single-digit fraction of the recovered mass, though the study notes that losses during filtration and precipitation remain material. The work frames next steps as scaling to kilogram feedstocks and validating performance across mixed scrap streams collected over 2024–2025.

A rare-earth-focused exchange-traded fund (ETF) recorded modest net outflows in April, according to data compiled from fund flows, as investors reduced exposure to the niche materials sector.

The ETF, which holds equities linked to rare-earth mining, processing and equipment, saw outflows that were smaller than the swings reported earlier in the year, suggesting limited risk-off positioning among retail and institutional participants. Separately, market participants pointed to ongoing cost and supply-chain pressures affecting the sector’s earnings outlook, factors that can influence ETF demand even when broader commodity sentiment remains stable. “April’s outflows were modest and consistent with investor timing around monthly flows, rather than a broad unwind,” one market participant said, adding that daily trading volumes stayed sufficient to keep spreads relatively steady. The participant did not name the investor or the fund.

In the ETF market overall, modest flow patterns are common in months when investors weigh macroeconomic data and interest-rate expectations, which can affect valuations for growth-oriented industrial sectors. For this fund, which is concentrated in a small universe of listed companies, even incremental changes in net subscriptions can translate into visible shifts in assets under management.

By the end of April, the ETF’s asset base had declined by a limited margin versus March, while performance remained driven largely by the movement of its underlying holdings rather than additional sponsor contributions. Analysts said the next key signal would be whether inflows return in May, particularly as investors adjust positions ahead of quarterly earnings updates from rare-earth-related companies and the latest updates on supply commitments.
a47c132e919d7b071ad4: Heavy rare earth elements generally refer to the lanthanide group from terbium through lutetium, a grouping used across mining, metallurgy, and downstream processing because their chemistry and separation difficulty differ from lighter counterparts. In most industry and academic usage, “heavy” starts at terbium (Tb) and includes dysprosium (Dy), holmium (Ho), erbium’s later boundary is sometimes contested by classification scheme, ytterbium (Yb), and lutetium (Lu). Trade and investment reports often align the label with high-demand permanent-magnet materials for NdFeB supply chains, where Dy and Tb are added in smaller but critical quantities to improve high-temperature coercivity. By contrast, “light” rare earths usually cover lanthanum, cerium, praseodymium, and neodymium. A practical threshold is not a single weight value but a set of sequential atomic numbers and oxide separation steps used in refining plants, where heavy fractions typically require more stages and higher purity specifications. Market watchers therefore track heavy baskets separately in pricing, because heavy oxides—often priced and supplied as separated products—remain more constrained than mixed rare-earth concentrates.
b63cc4e512a9a8305c41:
China has quietly locked in advantage across rare earths for decades.  
A new long-read traces control over mining, processing, magnets, and downstream refining.  
It highlights how Beijing built capacity when rivals treated the sector as a niche commodity.  
State planning, industrial scale, and a tight supply chain pushed Chinese products into global manufacturing.  
Even as Western firms diversified, key intermediate steps often still depended on Chinese processing.  
The article notes export curbs and permitting systems that can tighten markets quickly.  
The result is structural leverage, not just pricing power, through rare earth value chains.  
Diversification is underway, but timelines remain long and capital intensive.
fddc9228ca9314699f55:
A quarterly rare-earth podcast released by Argus Media on Friday reviewed recent developments across supply chains used to produce magnets and other critical materials, including mining output, processing capacity, pricing signals and logistics constraints.

The episode focused on how upstream supply has been reshaped by project timelines and capacity changes at separation and refining facilities. Analysts cited incremental gains in feedstock availability in China, alongside ongoing efforts to stabilise processing through expansion of solvent-extraction and rare-earth separation lines. They also said non-China capacity developments remain slow, with several projects continuing to face permitting, offtake and commissioning delays.

On the demand side, the roundup described varying order patterns for rare-earth products used in electric vehicles, wind power and industrial motors. It noted that magnet supply remains the key bottleneck for downstream users, since magnet production requires tightly controlled rare-earth grades and consistent rare-earth-to-metal ratios. The podcast said procurement strategies have continued to balance near-term needs with efforts to reduce exposure to volatility in dysprosium and terbium—elements commonly used to improve high-temperature performance in permanent magnets.

Argus also discussed pricing and contract dynamics, saying market participants are watching both spot indications and longer-term agreements. It said traders and manufacturers have increasingly tied contract terms to reference benchmarks, while buyers seek confirmation of material quality, traceability and impurity levels. Several segments referenced sensitivity in the market to shipping disruptions and changes in freight rates, which can affect the cadence of deliveries to processing hubs and magnet producers.

The episode further highlighted policy and trade measures that influence the movement of intermediate products and finished components. It pointed to compliance requirements for environmental reporting and export controls that can slow shipments of separated materials, and said companies are adjusting inventory buffers ahead of regulatory deadlines and seasonal transport windows.

In the podcast, one analyst said the most important near-term signal is whether separation capacity constraints ease “enough to convert upstream feedstock into consistent magnet-ready supply,” which would determine how quickly downstream lead times can improve. The quarterly roundup said it will continue tracking new capacity announcements, project milestones and any changes in demand from end-use sectors.
24bf5e2a184c14172354: A new rare-earth outlook published for 2030 projects a widening gap between neodymium demand and electric-vehicle sales growth, with implications for supply planning across 2026-2030. The chart framework estimates that EV sales rise steadily from 2025 levels through year-end 2030, while neodymium-linked consumption accelerates more unevenly, reflecting changing magnet intensity in traction motors and battery-pack mass. In the base case, neodymium requirements for EVs increase faster than vehicle deliveries in 2026 and 2027, then moderate during 2028-2030 as average magnet load per vehicle is assumed to improve and vehicle mix shifts toward models using lower rare-earth content per unit output. The same scenario highlights recurring risk around recycling rates and new separation capacity, particularly if commissioning schedules slip beyond typical 18-30 month lead times. Sensitivity bands in the publication suggest that a 5-10% shift in EV magnet intensity can move neodymium demand by a comparable margin by 2030, outweighing small changes in market volume. Overall, the chart points to tighter neodymium balancing needs through 2027 even if EV volumes continue expanding to 2030.
93c25dc5b41bd6627246:
The Pentagon maintains a rare-earth stockpile, but it is narrower than many headlines suggest.  
Its purpose is to keep critical defense supply chains running during disruptions, not to replace private mining.  
Rare earths are embedded in magnets, sensors, radar components, drones, precision-guided systems, and aircraft actuators.  
The stockpile is managed under programs that prioritize procurement for active military needs and industrial surge capacity.  
Funds support extraction, processing, and separation partners to reduce reliance on single foreign refining routes.  
Oversight focuses on readiness timelines, not commodity prices.  
The real bottleneck remains refining capacity, which stockpiling cannot fully solve.
787fbb053fe114fa62d8:
Markets for rare earths have reacted sharply to recent supply and pricing signals, and analysts say the move risks repeating past episodes in which prices overshot underlying fundamentals, according to commentary released by industry sources.

The discussion comes as buyers and traders reassess expectations for rare-earth products used in magnets, batteries and industrial motors. Several participants have pointed to changes in shipment schedules, producer guidance and China’s export policy implementation as drivers of near-term sentiment, while noting that actual flows can adjust more gradually than financial markets.

“Rare earths have a history of overreaction,” one market analyst said, citing earlier cycles in which prices surged on headlines before inventories, contracts and secondary supply absorbed the shock. The analyst said the market often finds a new equilibrium only after buyers revise restocking plans and when downstream customers confirm their demand outlook for finished components.

A key focus of the commentary is the balance between market liquidity and physical availability. While spot prices can move quickly when traders anticipate tighter supply, the availability of separated oxides, metals and magnet materials tends to depend on longer lead times across processing and refining steps. Industry sources said it can take weeks or months for disruptions or anticipated curbs to translate into sustained shortages at the levels where manufacturers actually purchase inputs.

The commentary also highlights that rare-earth markets are frequently influenced by policy-driven variables, including licensing and customs checks for export shipments. Even when policy signals are interpreted as restrictive, actual export volumes can remain steadier than expected because of existing contracts and logistical arrangements. Analysts said this can moderate price pressure once trade flows are reassessed.

Investors are watching whether current pricing dynamics reflect a temporary reaction to news events or a sustained change in supply-demand fundamentals. The commentary notes that downstream demand for permanent magnets, particularly in electric vehicles and wind power, typically evolves more slowly than commodity sentiment, and that inventory drawdowns by magnet and alloy producers can partially buffer immediate changes.

Producers and traders have continued to monitor the availability of key feedstocks, processing capacity and separation throughput. The commentary concludes that investors should consider whether recent market moves align with confirmed physical tightening, rather than relying solely on early signals, given the sector’s history of volatility around expectations.
6e4107c9774d59e0d1dd: A rare-earth analyst note released on 2026-03-08 said the sector’s price base case remains unchanged after a review of demand assumptions, inventory levels, and recent contract indicators across key magnet and alloy feedstocks. The update maintains 2026 full-year forecasts for major oxides and metals, citing broadly stable end-market purchasing patterns and limited evidence of a sustained shift in substitution rates for NdPr, dysprosium, and terbium compounds. It also retained its short-term view for European and Asian buyers, where spot liquidity has been mixed since late 2025, with lead times and freight costs continuing to influence near-term pricing. The note pointed to ongoing tightness in separated rare-earth processing capacity as the main constraint, with supply additions pushed out beyond the current quarter. While market participants expect more clarity after the next batch of procurement tenders in April 2026, the analyst said current indicators do not justify revisions to the base-case pricing curve.
a5cb3364b5d0df5a550e:
Rare-earth markets are expected to stay steady through 2027, according to sector tracking.  
Supply and demand should remain broadly balanced as new capacity comes online and ramp-ups are phased in.  
Price volatility is likely to be limited by cautious inventory behavior among buyers and continued scrutiny of contract terms.  
Producers face cost pressure from energy, refining reagents, and logistics, but margins should be cushioned by long-cycle supply agreements.  
Downstream demand will be driven by magnets for motors, wind, and electric vehicles, with slower consumer electronics growth offsetting part of the demand curve.  
Policy support in major producing and processing regions should help maintain investment momentum.
ac0f6813b9f4205f3ea6:
Lynas Rare Earths’ chief executive has outlined the company’s strategy to expand downstream processing and secure long-term supplies of critical minerals, in a rare profile interview that comes as governments push for more domestic rare-earth separation capacity.

In the interview, Lynas chief executive Amanda Lacaze said the company is focusing on the full value chain, from ore processing to separation and conversion, aiming to reduce reliance on third-country processing routes. She said Lynas intends to continue scaling output at its separation and conversion plants while maintaining strict operational controls to meet regulatory requirements.

Lacaze also addressed the availability of feedstock and the stability of supply for customers in the defence, electronics and clean-energy sectors. She said Lynas continues to work with suppliers to improve the consistency of rare-earth concentrates, while using offtake arrangements and customer partnerships to align production volumes with demand signals. She highlighted the need for predictable raw material flows and said the company is pursuing ways to lengthen its contract horizons to provide greater certainty for investors and end users.

On compliance and market access, Lacaze said the company’s projects are designed to meet environmental and safety standards in both operating jurisdictions. She referenced ongoing efforts to strengthen monitoring and reporting frameworks, particularly around tailings management and waste handling, while ensuring facilities remain compliant with permit conditions. She said these measures are central to building trust with regulators and local stakeholders.

The interview comes amid heightened global competition for rare-earths processing capacity, as China’s dominance in separation and refining has prompted policy moves in the United States, Europe and parts of Asia. Lacaze said Lynas sees opportunities in markets seeking alternatives to existing supply chains, adding that customer qualification timelines and technical requirements can be lengthy but are manageable with early engagement.

Looking ahead, she said Lynas plans to continue investing in capacity, operational efficiency and product portfolio development to serve demand for separated and converted materials used in magnets and other high-performance applications. She also said the company remains attentive to risks that can affect rare-earth supply and pricing, including logistics constraints and currency movements, and continues to adjust procurement and production plans accordingly.
c431e6b7cac62b2ac94e: Rare-earth markets are shaped by a narrow set of producers, long-cycle refining capacity, and a concentrated customer base for magnet and battery materials, according to an industry overview ahead of 2026 procurement seasons. Supply is dominated by China for rare-earth oxides and separated products, with market pricing typically tracking Chinese benchmark offers while downstream buyers face additional conversion and shipping costs. Demand is split between permanent magnets used in wind turbines and electric motors, and specialty applications in defense, lighting, and catalysts, with NdPr, dysprosium, and terbium the most strategically watched commodities. Refining and separation lead times commonly run from 6 to 24 months, limiting quick reactions to spot shortages or regulatory changes. Contracts in the sector frequently mix term volumes for NdPr and dysprosium-rich streams with spot adjustments, reflecting both yield variability and price volatility. Industry participants said inventory cycles can last 1 to 2 quarters for magnet feedstock, while end-user restocking after disruptions often extends into 2027 given qualification timelines for alloys and finished components.
685510a420b0180f6b41:

The company said it expects performance to track current market assumptions, without signaling a sharp upside or downside.

ee5fd84d6d977383869f:
Rare-earth miner Lynas Rare Earths reported steady production guidance, saying output across its processing network is expected to remain broadly in line with recent levels despite ongoing market volatility.

In an update to investors on Tuesday, the company said it continues to anticipate a stable run-rate for the separation of rare-earth products, with volumes expected to support contracted demand for materials used in magnets for electric vehicles, wind turbines and other applications. The company’s guidance covers output from its processing facilities, including its main Lynas Advanced Materials (LAM) operation in Malaysia, as well as contributions from earlier-stage operations that feed the processing chain. Lynas also pointed to progress on operational reliability. The company said maintenance planning is aligned with the production outlook and that it expects to manage throughput without material disruptions. It did not provide new capital expenditure figures in the update, but noted that spending remains directed toward sustaining processing performance and addressing regulatory and operational requirements at its facilities.

“The continued stability in our production guidance reflects the performance of our plants and our ability to manage key inputs and operational schedules,” Lynas said in the statement. The company added that it expects commodity prices and customer purchasing decisions to remain drivers of quarterly revenue and margin performance, although production itself is not currently forecast to shift.

Rare-earth miners have faced fluctuating demand tied to the timing of new magnet and component projects, as well as pricing dynamics influenced by both Chinese supply and policy measures. Lynas, which supplies material for downstream manufacturers, has previously emphasized the importance of long-term offtake agreements to smooth demand swings and support production planning.

11c15bbd2da5c310a485: Rare-earth exchange-traded funds recorded marginally positive inflows month-to-date as of 10 March 2026, according to data compiled from listed products tracking the sector. Market participants cited positioning ahead of upcoming industrial-policy and procurement updates, with risk appetite constrained by currency moves and refining-cost expectations.
16df6fa026a3beac3c07:
A rare-earth demand modelling debate played out at a mining conference this week, with analysts arguing over how quickly buyers will scale up magnet and battery supply.  
Several speakers said forecasts are too optimistic because vehicle adoption, turbine deployments, and electronics demand all move with policy cycles and raw-material pricing.  
Others pushed back, arguing that tightening supply outside China and new magnet production in North America will pull demand forward.  
The discussion also turned to scrap recycling assumptions and whether substitutes for specific oxides can reduce long-term volumes.  
Investors were watching for guidance on contracts, pricing floors, and new mine timelines.
bd038a02e1206883a37f:
A rare-earth focused company on Wednesday held an “investor day” briefing in which it restated its long-term targets, seeking to reinforce expectations for future capacity and cash generation amid ongoing demand for cleaner manufacturing inputs.

Management reiterated the company’s multi-year plan for scaling production of separated rare-earth materials and expanding downstream processing, following prior disclosures that had placed milestones on a longer timeline. The company said it expects progress to be driven by continued project execution, contracted offtake arrangements and efficiencies aimed at lowering unit costs over time.

At the briefing, the firm outlined updated assumptions used to set its forward targets, including changes in sourcing and processing yields, pricing for rare-earth products and the timing of ramp-ups across its operations. It also highlighted work underway to stabilize feedstock supplies and reduce disruptions associated with permitting, construction and industrial commissioning steps that can affect development schedules.

Investors were also given details on the company’s approach to securing demand, including efforts to align production plans with customer specifications for permanent magnets and other industrial applications. The company said it continues to prioritize customers in sectors such as electric vehicles, wind power and industrial motors, where rare-earth usage is expected to remain structurally supported.

In its long-term outlook, the company maintained its targets for key performance metrics, including an expected increase in annual rare-earth output and a path to stronger margins as production matures. It said the targets are based on the assumption that the industry environment remains sufficiently supportive and that major project components meet the planned commissioning sequence.

“Restating these targets is important for investors as we move from development into the next phase of scaled operations,” a senior executive said during the presentation. The executive added that the company is focused on execution against schedule and on meeting quality requirements needed for qualification by magnet and alloy customers.

The company’s investor day comes as rare-earth producers globally face a mix of opportunities and constraints, including shifting government policies on critical minerals, tighter compliance requirements and fluctuations in downstream demand. While the company did not provide a new near-term forecast, it said it remains on track for interim updates tied to project milestones later in the year.
ddbad97294b773014d2d: A new scholarship fund dedicated to rare-earth metallurgy students was launched on March 11, 2026, with initial funding of $25 million aimed at supporting undergraduate and graduate training. The program will offer 120 scholarships over the next 4 academic years, targeting students in courses covering rare-earth extraction, separation, alloy development and magnet materials. Disbursements are planned at $12,000 per year for undergraduate recipients and $28,000 per year for graduate fellows, with additional $3,500 stipends for laboratory placements. Eligible applicants must enroll at participating institutions in Europe and North America that are conducting rare-earth processing research, and selections will be made based on academic performance and documented experience in materials engineering. The fund also includes a 6-month industry internship track beginning in September 2026, prioritizing placements with producers and refiners. Organizers said the first cohort will be notified by June 30, 2026, and all recipients will begin coursework in the fall semester.
3d101b91245dde795e54:
A rare-earth trade mission attended a Tokyo summit focused on critical minerals this week, reflecting growing competition for materials used in batteries, magnets, and defense systems.  
Delegates discussed supply security, export restrictions, and new investment routes linking mines, processing, and recycling.  
Japanese officials said the summit aimed to accelerate partnerships across government, automakers, and technology firms.  
Participants also reviewed standards for traceability and sustainability to reduce risk of disruptions.  
The mission highlighted the importance of rare-earth processing capacity outside China, where most separation and refining is concentrated.  
Organizers said follow-up work will target joint procurement, research collaboration, and financing mechanisms for new projects.
f381bd298a2e28d85d10:
A panel at a rare-earths conference on Thursday focused on the economics of recycling, weighing whether collection, processing and policy incentives can make secondary supply competitive with newly mined materials.

The session brought together industry and academic speakers to examine the cost drivers that determine how much rare-earth metal can realistically be recovered from end-of-life products such as magnets, batteries and catalysts. Participants said that while recycling technologies are improving, the economics often hinge on the ability to sort waste streams reliably and on the price volatility of primary rare-earths, which can alter investment timelines for new recycling capacity.

Speakers said recycling performance varies widely depending on the product type and the form of the metals involved. Magnet scrap, for example, can provide higher recoverable concentrations than diluted waste streams, but it also requires specialized collection systems and downstream metallurgical steps. Panelists also discussed the energy and chemical inputs needed to separate mixed rare-earths, noting that facility design and feedstock composition can strongly affect yields and operating costs.

One speaker highlighted that regulatory and market mechanisms can shift the balance. “Without stable demand signals or procurement pathways for recycled rare-earth materials, recyclers struggle to finance the scale-up needed to reduce costs,” the panel’s chair said, referring to government targets for critical minerals and growing requirements for supply-chain traceability in electronics and electric vehicles.

Participants also addressed the role of design-for-recycling, including material choices and product architectures that can make magnets and alloys easier to recover at end of life. They said that changes at the manufacturing stage can reduce the cost of disassembly and improve the purity of recovered rare-earth fractions, which in turn affects downstream separation and refining expenses.

The panelists concluded that recycling economics are likely to improve gradually as collection systems mature and as more manufacturers build in recycled content requirements. They also said that planners need better data on rare-earth flows, from scrap generation to processing and final re-use, to guide investment decisions and policy support. The conference, held over multiple days, is expected to continue discussions on critical minerals supply chains and industrial decarbonization efforts tied to mineral processing.
8a11744d455222e7be33: A rare-earth producer has renewed an offtake agreement with an Asian magnet manufacturer, extending supply for high-performance neodymium-iron-boron magnet production, according to the companies’ filings. The renewed contract covers quantities tied to quarterly production schedules from the producer’s major processing and separation sites in 2025 and 2026, with shipments expected to start in the second half of 2026. Pricing is linked to benchmark rare-earth levels and adjusts monthly based on spot indices for NdPr and dysprosium-related inputs, according to the agreement summary. Delivery terms include penalties tied to missed lot sizes and a provision for volume flexibility, allowing both parties to reallocate up to 10% of scheduled tonnage during off-peak periods. The magnet maker, which supplies motors for industrial drives and electric mobility, said the renewal supports long-term demand planning amid ongoing supply tightness for dysprosium and terbium-heavy mixes. The producer has also pledged additional working-capital support for ramp-up purchases of feedstock ahead of the 2026 compliance window for emissions and waste-handling requirements.
fe7add89b27227f22d4a:
A rare-earth industry lobby group has published a package of policy recommendations aimed at securing more reliable supplies of critical metals.  
The group urged governments to diversify sourcing and expand domestic processing, arguing that refining capacity is the bottleneck behind current price swings.  
It called for faster permitting for mines and separation plants, with clearer timelines and reduced regulatory uncertainty.  
The recommendations also emphasize strategic stockpiles, longer-term offtake contracts, and incentives for recycling and recovery from end-of-life electronics.  
The group wants export controls and trade restrictions coordinated across allied markets to limit sudden disruptions.  
Officials said the proposals could feed into upcoming industrial and energy security plans.
af123cd9e574cfd08575:
A widely shared chart tracking rare-earth prices using a 90-day moving average was posted online on March 14, providing a smoothed view of recent market trends for key elements used in magnets and high-tech manufacturing.

The chart, circulated through a Telegram channel focused on rare-earth developments, presents a “90-day moving average” for multiple rare-earth products. Moving averages are used in financial reporting to reduce short-term volatility and highlight broader direction over roughly three months. The post framed the latest reading as a snapshot of how prices have been trending across the period, rather than a day-to-day assessment.

Data displayed in the chart included a range of compounds and separation products, typically including widely followed elements such as neodymium, praseodymium, dysprosium and terbium, according to the categories shown in the graphic. The values were presented as recent average levels calculated over the preceding 90 days, with the lines or data points updated to reflect the most recent observations available to the chart’s compiler.

In related commentary accompanying the chart, the Telegram channel pointed to the moving average as an indicator of momentum in the rare-earth market and noted that the measure can lag rapid changes. Such lags are common when markets experience abrupt supply, demand or pricing shifts, as three-month smoothing averages those swings across a longer window.

The rare-earth sector has been closely watched by manufacturers of permanent magnets, electric-drive systems and electronics components, where the availability and pricing of certain heavy rare earths can affect production planning. International prices are influenced by factors including export controls, mining output, downstream demand and inventory levels held by suppliers and consumers.

Market participants typically monitor both spot assessments and longer-running indicators to gauge stability. While spot prices can move quickly on limited transactions, moving averages are often used to interpret the direction of change over time and to compare current levels with earlier periods. The March 14 posting added another update to that monitoring cycle, showing the latest 90-day averages for the elements included in the tracker.

The chart’s underlying data sources were not detailed in the Telegram post, but the graphic’s presentation suggested it is based on regularly observed pricing benchmarks. The update was shared within the rare-earth research and news community and was reposted by followers seeking a consolidated view of price trends.
b2feb2d2478dba634ca8: Satellite monitoring tied to DR rare-earth supply-chain indicators detected congestion in dysprosium–rare-earth (DR) logistics routes consistent with a typical spread pattern, according to a report published 15 March 2026. The assessment found that DR-related vessel scheduling tightened across three major transit corridors between 02 and 14 March, with average dwell times rising from a baseline of 18–22 hours to 27–33 hours at affected nodes. Congestion peaked on 08 March, when aggregated throughput fell by 6.4% compared with the prior 30-day median. Spatial analysis showed the bottlenecks were distributed rather than concentrated, matching historical “typical spread” behavior seen during routine port surges. Monitoring also flagged intermittent equipment downtime at two transshipment sites, but no single failure accounted for more than 1.8% of total delay hours. The report expects normalization by 21 March if no new restrictions or weather disruptions emerge along the corridors.
f0791a6381ffc067f559:
Rare-earth supply indicators stayed stable as LME warehouse stocks showed little movement.  
Authorized holdings for key materials were largely unchanged over the latest review period.  
Some product lines saw minor inflows, but they were offset by routine withdrawals.  
Market participants described the change as maintenance trading rather than a new demand signal.  
Forward pricing attention appears to be shifting back to broader policy and industrial activity expectations.  
Traders will be watching for signs of tightening if withdrawals accelerate faster than replenishment.  
For now, the warehouse tape is sending a steady message to buyers managing near-term sourcing risk.
866a7a423114aff19547:
A social media post about rare-earth prices circulated widely on the messaging platform Telegram on March 15, drawing attention to routine market commentary but offering no new policy or supply-chain developments, according to the account that published it.

The post, shared by the Telegram channel “Rare Earth Watch,” said the latest movement in rare-earth markets reflected ongoing buying and pricing adjustments rather than any abrupt shift in production or demand. It pointed to steady interest from buyers in light rare-earth compounds and reiterated that spot quotes were being shaped mainly by incremental contract pricing and availability of specific material grades.

The channel did not name particular companies or cite official transaction data. It framed the commentary as an update for readers tracking rare-earth prices and supply conditions, and it referenced broader market expectations for demand linked to downstream sectors such as magnets, electric motors and energy-related equipment.

Traders and analysts said that in markets for rare earths, short-term price discussions on social media are often based on limited information and can lag behind formal pricing mechanisms. In recent months, market participants have relied on a mix of spot transactions, contract renegotiations and export flows when assessing near-term pricing, they said, while longer-term moves are more closely tied to capacity changes, policy signals and inventory levels.

One rare-earth buyer contacted for this report said commentary of the type shared on the Telegram channel typically aligns with how procurement teams monitor the market but said decisions usually depend on confirmations from suppliers and the availability of specific separated or processed materials. “We look at what the supplier can deliver and at what grade and lead time,” the buyer said. “Social updates may reflect sentiment, but order timing comes from practical supply.”

As of publication, there were no accompanying announcements about new mining output, refined separation capacity or changes to export controls. The post also did not include links to regulatory documents or financial filings, and it did not report any disruptions in logistics or shipping that could have affected pricing.

Rare-earth prices remain sensitive to fluctuations in demand for high-performance magnets and to changes in inventories held by producers, traders and industrial end users. Market watchers said that, absent new fundamentals, routine commentary posts generally serve as signals of sentiment rather than indicators of an immediate price shock.
2e468ce1f6ff080e9fac: Rare-earth pricing in the monitored market showed little month-on-month change, according to data compiled by Rare Earth Watch, covering the period ending 2026-03-15. Compared with February 2026, the average price delta across tracked grades and oxides was minimal, with most items posting single-digit percentage moves over a roughly 28-day window. Several dysprosium and terbium-linked contracts were reported as stable, while neodymium-praseodymium oxides edged slightly lower in the latest assessment, consistent with subdued spot demand. Praseodymium and neodymium separation feedstock prices remained near prior levels, limiting cost pass-through to end products. The bulletin also noted that supply disruptions were not prominent during the month, reducing volatility in monthly settlement ranges. Overall, the dataset indicated that buyers and sellers continued to reference recent benchmark bands rather than resetting prices, leaving the market largely range-bound into mid-March 2026.
57a61e31901a6380deb7:
Lithium spot prices fell around 6% after reports showed higher Chinese inventories.  
Traders said the increase in stockpiles reduced immediate demand for prompt material.  
Producers and buyers shifted toward waiting for better price levels and narrower spreads.  
Market participants noted that sentiment also reflected thinner buying interest ahead of near-term contract restocking cycles.  
While underlying demand for battery-grade lithium remains supported by EV plans, spot pricing is being driven by inventory timing.  
Dealers expect volatility to persist until inventory drawdowns or renewed restocking triggers a clearer direction.
ee013c50b31f6e8dcd57:
Cobalt prices gained as battery manufacturers showed renewed buying interest, market participants said, after recent volatility in the metal’s supply chain eased.

Dealers reported that buyers linked to lithium-ion battery production increased inquiries for cobalt units used in cathode materials, particularly for higher-spec batteries where the metal remains important for energy density. The improved sentiment came after a period in which consumers scaled back procurement schedules amid uncertainty over near-term demand and processor capacity for refining.

A trader at a European metals distributor said the renewed activity was concentrated in spot and short-term volumes rather than longer-dated contracts, as buyers sought to replenish inventories while prices stabilized. “We’re seeing more active bidding than in the last couple of weeks, and several customers are looking to secure near-term supply for manufacturing,” the trader said.

Producers and intermediaries said the market’s narrower spread between offers and bids suggested sellers were more willing to transact at prevailing levels. Some suppliers indicated they were drawing down excess warehouse stock to fulfill confirmed orders, while others maintained tighter terms for deliveries tied to specific quality grades and chemical specifications. Industry sources also pointed to continuing constraints in refining and intermediate processing as a factor limiting the availability of certain cobalt outputs.

On the demand side, analysts tied the increased buying interest to gradual normalization across parts of the battery supply chain, including cathode production planning and logistics scheduling. Battery makers have increasingly targeted production continuity for electric vehicles and consumer electronics, and this has translated into more frequent procurement discussions with cobalt suppliers.

The cobalt market also remains sensitive to broader pricing drivers, including nickel and lithium dynamics and moves in the Chinese raw materials and refining sectors. While buyers appear to be re-entering the market, participants said the pace of purchasing could remain uneven depending on how battery producers manage production schedules and whether cost pressures ease or intensify.

By late trading, market participants said liquidity improved modestly as more offers met bids, though they cautioned that flows could change quickly if currency moves, refining availability, or buyer forecasts shift.
ccc5bc6581d531469c5a: Nickel prices are pressured by oversupply that is expected to persist through year-end, according to market participants tracking recent balance-sheet updates. Inventories at warehouses and in exchange-linked channels remain elevated after a weaker-than-expected drawdown in Q4 2025, leaving the market with a surplus that analysts estimate at several thousand tonnes. Refinery output in the first months of 2026 has held near steady levels, while demand signals from stainless steel and battery supply chains have not improved enough to absorb current deliveries. Additional nickel could also enter the market via incremental shipments tied to ramp-ups in Indonesia and other suppliers, prolonging tightness in supply growth rather than demand recovery. Several forecasts now point to a net surplus continuing into the fourth quarter of 2026, with volatility likely around restocking cycles and changes in freight costs. Dealers said physical premiums have softened while liquidity remains sufficient, reflecting the risk of continued offloading ahead of seasonal demand peaks.
57dd65144433ab6e1ab2:
Copper on the LME is testing the $9,500 per tonne resistance level as traders weigh near-term supply risks against weakening demand signals.  
Spot and front-month interest picked up after prices held recent support and began pressing higher in thin liquidity conditions.  
Market watchers are also tracking strikes and disruptions outside traditional hubs, which can tighten available inventories.  
At the same time, buyers are more selective, reflecting concerns about slower industrial activity and stronger-than-expected substitution in some end markets.  
A clean break above $9,500 would likely trigger short covering and fresh spread trades.  
Failure to hold would reopen downside toward the last consolidation band.
8c1178c548dff083ef06:
The tin market tightened further as Indonesia continued to ship fewer volumes, keeping supplies constrained for buyers, according to trading sources and market assessments released this week.

Demand for tin remained supported by ongoing procurement ahead of seasonal production schedules, market participants said, while near-term availability continued to narrow. Several dealers reported that sales were being concentrated in smaller lots, with sellers citing limited cargo space and slower dispatch timelines compared with earlier periods.

Indonesia, the world’s largest tin producer, has been a key swing factor for global flows. Traders said export schedules have been irregular, reflecting operational and logistical constraints as well as the pace at which concentrates and refined metal are converted and cleared for shipment. As a result, some customers have had to extend delivery dates or source alternatives, which reduced the overall pool of readily available material.

In the physical market, bid-ask spreads reportedly widened as buyers competed for supply and sellers were more selective on pricing and delivery terms. One trader said tighter conditions were most evident in cargoes for prompt dates, where offers were less frequent and premiums were more pronounced. “The market is finding it hard to clear inventory quickly, so prompt availability is staying firm,” the trader said.

On the pricing side, assessments indicated support for tin throughout the forward curve, consistent with a market adjusting to a smaller supply base. Participants noted that financing costs and exchange inventory levels continue to influence sentiment, but that Indonesian export volumes have remained the dominant driver of short-term tightness.

Looking ahead, market expectations hinge on whether Indonesian shipments pick up in the coming weeks and how steadily producers can convert and dispatch material. Traders said any improvement in export throughput could ease pressure, but that buyers are likely to remain cautious until they see sustained increases in confirmed cargoes. Further movement in the market will also depend on customer restocking patterns and the availability of substitute supplies from other producing regions.
9bda5035221d02fe0daf: Graphite suppliers are warning that third-quarter supply will remain tight in the flake market, with availability constraints expected to persist through at least end-June 2026, according to industry contacts. Several vendors said contracted volumes for natural and synthetic flake are tracking below demand signals from battery and industrial customers, leaving lead times longer than typical for Q2 replenishments. One concern highlighted by suppliers is the mismatch between fixed offtake schedules and spot requirements, which has widened the gap between quoted availability and sellable material. Pricing pressure is likely to remain skewed toward sellers, they said, as inventories at processing sites are reportedly being managed for incremental releases rather than bulk shipments. Suppliers also pointed to continued operating limits tied to energy and refining throughput, which can reduce output conversion rates for coated and high-purity flake lines. Market participants expect additional tightening effects into Q4 if new qualification cycles for battery-grade grades do not release additional tonnage by September 2026.
8e3004145a47f6723cf8:
Platinum and palladium prices diverged as market participants reassessed how auto demand will split across the two metals.  
Platinum tracked steadier expectations for gasoline-related use and industrial recovery, but traders said sentiment stayed cautious ahead of fresh consumption data.  
Palladium moved on a different pricing impulse, with investors watching substitution trends in three-way catalysts and the pace of switching back toward platinum where contracts allow.  
Dealers highlighted thinner near-term liquidity for palladium, amplifying moves on positioning and any incremental changes in offtake guidance.  
Analysts also pointed to divergent stock drawdown signals from refiners and wholesalers.
63cffde9cbc6b0bff369:
Tungsten spot premiums increased for European buyers this week, as tighter availability of the metal pushed sellers to ask higher prices for immediate delivery.

Traders said the premium for tungsten on the spot market widened on the back of reduced prompt supply and continued demand from manufacturers seeking to secure input ahead of scheduled production runs. Several market participants cited near-term restocking needs, particularly among firms that have been drawing from limited inventories following periods of firmer upstream conditions.

In Europe, buyers reported having to pay more for material delivered quickly, with offers reflecting a wider spread between spot levels and contract-based pricing. The change comes after weeks in which premiums had been comparatively stable, although dealer discussions indicate that the spot market had already started to price in higher procurement costs.

One rare and specialty metals distributor said the movement was driven largely by “short lead times and fewer available offers,” adding that the gap between spot and longer-term deals has been widening as sellers prioritize deliveries that can be confirmed quickly. The distributor also noted that buyers seeking specific grades or packaging formats were more exposed to the limited availability, which can raise effective premiums even when base metal levels are steady.

Market sources also pointed to logistical factors affecting near-term trading. Shipments planned for the coming weeks can influence how quickly sellers are able to clear stock, and delays in upstream processing can concentrate supply availability into narrower windows. As a result, spot pricing has tended to react more sharply to day-to-day changes in offer volumes than contract prices.

Despite the premium increase, participants said the underlying demand picture for tungsten end uses, including hardmetals and cemented carbide applications, remained broadly supported. Several buyers said they were balancing spot procurement with scheduled purchases, but that the urgency of some orders has increased reliance on spot supply.

Dealers expect the premium trend to remain sensitive to prompt availability in the coming sessions, particularly if more offers come to market or if upstream production adjustments tighten supply further. For now, European buyers have faced a higher cost of securing tungsten for immediate delivery compared with recent weeks.
a8822c44ea6375c8fdf0: Vanadium prices stabilised after a first-quarter drawdown, according to market reports on Thursday, as trading liquidity improved following weeks of weaker demand signals. Benchmark offers for vanadium products steadied in mid-March after falling from January highs, with the latest quotations broadly flat week-on-week as buyers returned to the market in smaller, more consistent lots. Analysts cited lower inventory clearances by producers during late February and early March as a key factor behind the earlier slide, which reflected cautious restocking by steelmakers and alloy fabricators. Spot activity picked up around mid-quarter, with participants pointing to tighter availability for certain grades and delayed shipments from several sourcing regions. Pricing stability is expected to persist into late March, though traders flagged that crude steel production plans for April and May could quickly change buying patterns. Market watchers said any renewed volatility would likely be driven by further changes in ferro-vanadium consumption at blast furnaces, as well as FX moves affecting import costs.
6981ed8682b1c69294ff:
Gallium prices moved in a balanced range after fresh German scrap flows resumed this week.  
Dealers said recycling supply improved enough to offset softer buying interest from parts of the semiconductor supply chain.  
Participants noted that secondary material has been the key swing factor, particularly as refiners look to keep feedstock stable through near-term contracting.  
Spot activity remained selective, with buyers focusing on immediate delivery lots rather than longer coverage.  
Market sentiment stayed cautious ahead of further assessments of industrial demand and potential changes in scrap availability from European collectors.  
Overall, liquidity improved slightly, but no clear directional pressure emerged.
f46eafac7a4901db319b:
Indium prices rose to a two-year high as stronger demand for semiconductors tightened supply, according to market participants.

In the latest trading, indium climbed to its highest level in about two years, reflecting increased ordering activity tied to chip manufacturing and related electronics, where the metal is used for conductive and soldering applications. Traders said the market has been supported by firmer forward buying and lower availability of some refined material.

Dealers attributed the move primarily to the semiconductor sector’s continued ramp-up, which has lifted consumption of specialty metals used in thin-film coatings and display and packaging components. Several producers have also faced constraints in delivering incremental volumes, leaving buyers to compete more aggressively for spot supply, they said.

The upward pressure has been compounded by expectations of continued tightness in the indium market into the second quarter. While other end-use segments such as flat-panel displays have been more variable, participants said the bulk of recent demand has come from semiconductor supply chains seeking stable input costs and longer contract coverage.

One source at a trading firm said tightness in refined inventories has become more visible in the spot market, with sellers offering smaller quantities and at higher price levels. “Premiums have widened as buyers look to secure material for semiconductor production schedules,” the person said.

Market data also pointed to higher liquidity, with more transactions reported at the top of the range. Analysts said the sharpness of the move suggests participants were adjusting quickly to new balance-sheet information on inventories and demand forecasts, rather than building positions gradually over multiple sessions.

Despite the gains, traders cautioned that the market’s trajectory could hinge on how quickly higher prices influence restocking cycles and on the timing of additional supply from smelters and refiners. They said any release of inventories or slower ordering from electronics manufacturers could limit further upside, even as semiconductor-related consumption remains a key driver.
48f69f51f312d47ebb1f: Molybdenum prices held steady after reports on March 20 that a Chinese production disruption could tighten global supply, according to market participants tracking spot activity and long-term contract indicators. Assessments showed the outage news was not reflected in immediate buying requirements, with traders pointing to inventories in secondary markets and ongoing shipments through early April. Fast markets data cited in industry feeds indicated that seller offers for molybdenum concentrate and molybdenum metal continued to clear in normal volumes despite the disruption narrative, with premiums remaining within a narrow range versus late-February levels. In China, production schedules affected by localized maintenance were expected to be offset by higher output from adjacent facilities, limiting the impact on overall availability. As of March 21, benchmark import reference levels showed no significant repricing, and forward deals for April delivery were reported to be priced on previous spreads. Traders said broader risk remains linked to seasonal demand from steel and alloy producers, but the near-term market response to the outage was muted.
0c03b980fe1a412c728e:
Uranium spot prices are testing the $110 level as traders weigh fresh supply anxieties.  
Buyers are showing renewed interest amid concerns about slower deliveries and tighter term coverage.  
Market participants point to uneven production schedules and distribution bottlenecks that could influence near-term availability.  
Spot liquidity is thinner than usual, amplifying price sensitivity to incremental news.  
Several counterparties are also recalibrating restocking plans ahead of future contract negotiations.  
Traders expect volatility to persist until clearer signals emerge on output timing and inventory releases.  
Any improvement in supply visibility could cap gains quickly, but downside risk is limited while uncertainty remains elevated.
01fc497eb86f0884fe9b:
Cadmium prices were steady on Thursday in a relatively thin trading environment, with participants reporting limited liquidity and fewer active bids across key delivery periods.

In most benchmark assessments, cash offers and bid levels were largely unchanged from the prior session, according to market sources. Traders said the subdued activity reflected a wait-and-see attitude ahead of upcoming demand signals from downstream users, alongside cautious restocking schedules that have kept volumes lower than usual for this time of year.

Several participants pointed to tighter concentration of activity around specific contract windows, rather than broad-based trading across the curve. That pattern left some levels supported by sell-side restraint, while buyers were selective, focusing on smaller quantities and specific grades. One dealer described the market as “quiet, with spreads not widening much but not tightening either,” adding that many players were reluctant to commit size without clearer indications on near-term consumption.

On the supply side, market participants said physical availability remained adequate, with sellers more willing to hold inventory rather than reduce prices in low-volume conditions. At the same time, there were no widespread reports of disruptions in production or logistics that would typically force repricing. Dealers also noted that financing costs and currency movements had not shifted significantly enough to alter pricing sentiment during the session.

In broader metals markets, sentiment remained mixed, but cadmium trading did not appear to track any major directional moves in related commodities on the day. Traders said hedging demand was limited, and that the absence of fresh market-moving news contributed to the lack of volatility. Several also highlighted that bids tended to cluster near assessed ranges, which helped keep quoted prices stable even as turnover stayed light.

By the close, market sources indicated that participants continued to monitor offers and potential changes in delivery schedules, though no clear catalyst emerged to prompt a sustained shift in pricing. Thin liquidity, they said, was likely to persist until more buyers return to the market or until new orders provide stronger confirmation of demand.
7e0e5dd0bf482882bc02: Premiums for selenium grades in Asia edged lower as Korean buying slowed, according to market participants on 21 March. Traded prices for premium selenium were assessed at tighter differentials versus benchmark levels after demand from South Korean converters eased following earlier restocking in February. Several spot deals were reported at reduced premiums amid softer inquiries tied to lower utilization rates at electronics-related processing units. Sellers said availability improved in the short term, adding to pressure to narrow spreads. In the assessment week, market liquidity remained limited, with most business concentrated in near-term April delivery windows. Analysts noted that Chinese producers held more stable base offers, but buyers shifted toward contracted volumes and postponed incremental purchases. The change left selenium premium levels moving closer to the mid-range of recent weeks, though buyers continued to monitor supply schedules for potential firming if lead times lengthened.
62b304590e3e452408c2:
Bismuth shipments are set to face higher costs after China announced an export tax increase.  
The duty will rise by 10% effective in May, according to trade and policy signals being tracked by market participants.  
The move is expected to tighten supply flows for downstream processors outside China.  
It could also push up contract prices for bismuth-containing products, where margins are already sensitive to rare-metal volatility.  
Traders will likely adjust sourcing and hedging plans ahead of the new tariff schedule.  
Any quick reaction in spot markets will depend on stock availability and the pace of export licensing under the revised regime.
a6a30539c0f5afa48568:
Antimony prices firmed this week as new sanctions and tighter compliance requirements curtailed exports from Russia, traders and buyers said, supporting higher offers for the metal used in flame retardants, alloys and batteries.

The rally comes after Russian producers faced increased restrictions on shipments and payment channels, limiting the availability of some grades in Asia and Europe. Several buyers reported longer lead times and higher freight-related costs when ordering from Russian-linked supply, prompting them to look to alternative sources or accept price increases from existing contracts.

In the spot market, antimony sellers lifted quotations on improving demand for refined material and for stocks that can be delivered quickly. Market participants said spot premiums widened most for three-nines and four-nines antimony trioxide feedstock, which is commonly used in pigment and chemical applications. Some traders also cited reduced inventories among intermediaries as distributors replenished ahead of expected supply disruptions.

“Sanctions are changing the logistics and documentation burden, and that is showing up in availability,” said one metals trader who deals with both refining and distribution, adding that “the market is pricing that risk into spot.”

Despite the price gains, deals were described as selective, with buyers focusing on contract volumes and limiting speculative purchases. Several manufacturers said they were assessing end-of-quarter demand and considering whether existing inventories can cover production needs, which has constrained how far prices can move in the near term.

Analysts said the supply response from outside Russia is likely to be gradual. While antimony is produced in a number of countries, output expansions and new offtake arrangements typically require lead times, and alternative suppliers may prioritize existing customer relationships. Buyers also noted that the quality range and processing routes for antimony can vary, affecting substitution decisions for specific industrial uses.

Trade flows for refined antimony and antimony-bearing compounds remain sensitive to enforcement actions and bank compliance, traders said. Payments and customs clearance have become slower for some lanes, and brokers have been more reluctant to guarantee shipment terms where documentation is uncertain. That has kept the market tight enough for prices to rise, they said, even as demand signals remain mixed.
c467440116cb886985d8: A Germanium secondary smelter has announced scheduled maintenance that will reduce output next month, according to an internal notice circulated to customers. Production at the facility is set to be adjusted starting 2026-04-06, with the planned work period extending through 2026-04-19. The operator said feedstock receipts and conversion runs will be staggered during the shutdown window, affecting both recovery of metal and refining throughput. Inventory shipments are expected to be prioritized ahead of the start date, while orders scheduled to load during the maintenance period may face revised delivery dates. The company did not indicate whether additional lines would be kept in standby or restarted in phases, but it flagged normal operations resuming on or around 2026-04-20. Market participants are watching the impact on short-cycle spot availability of secondary germanium material, particularly for April contracts already tied to tight refining schedules.
a5c2e208b983c7ad95f7:
Beryllium prices edged higher after US defense demand stayed firm into the spring bidding cycle.  
Quotes moved up as buyers signaled tighter control of supply for aerospace and military applications.  
Market participants cited limited availability of qualified material and a slower ramp-up of upstream processing.  
Producers indicated that lead times are still a factor for specialty grades used in high-performance components.  
Dealers said activity is concentrated in contracts tied to ongoing procurement schedules rather than broad spot buying.  
While broader industrial demand remains mixed, defense-linked tenders are providing a steadier floor for pricing.  
Any additional schedule changes in US programs could quickly impact next-round quotes.
e761ba627d7c73a5ef4f:
The zirconium market was quiet on Tuesday as buyers held back ahead of expected orders from electric vehicle original equipment manufacturers (OEMs), traders said.

Physical activity remained subdued across key trading hubs, with limited spot volumes exchanged and most market participants focusing on contract negotiations and delivery scheduling for later in the quarter. Several suppliers said they were monitoring demand signals from EV supply chains, where zirconium demand is tied to components used in batteries and high-performance ceramics, as well as to wider industrial uses.

Prices in the spot market were largely unchanged, with deal ranges reported in narrow bands rather than broad repricing. Traders said the lack of urgency was linked to timing: many buyers prefer to align purchases with confirmed production plans from OEMs, particularly when inventories are adequate for near-term requirements. As a result, participants have been extending lead times or shifting to term arrangements rather than adding incremental spot quantities.

On the supply side, concerns were mainly focused on logistics and milling availability rather than on metal feedstock shortages. Several sellers noted that downstream processing slots for zirconium compounds and intermediate products can tighten ahead of major order cycles, prompting a cautious approach to new offers. While some mills have maintained steady operating rates, distributors said they were receiving fewer inquiries than usual for this time of year.

Market participants also pointed to a broader pattern of cautious procurement in industrial minerals and materials linked to EV capex. With OEM orders expected to firm up over coming weeks, buyers are likely to revisit procurement targets once forecasts are finalized. “We’re seeing buyers stay conservative until they get clearer confirmation on OEM call-offs,” one trader at a European brokerage said. “Most of the business is being pushed into the next order window rather than executed immediately.”

Analysts said liquidity could improve quickly if confirmed EV-related purchase orders begin to filter through to converters and end users. They added that any shift in import schedules, shipping costs, or regulatory documentation could also influence near-term availability and transaction volumes. For now, traders expect trading conditions to remain rangebound until the next wave of OEM guidance.
164b3a5f169949dddc3e: Lithium miners are projecting slightly higher output for the second quarter, according to guidance updates published through 2026-03-24. Several producers raised planned volumes by small increments, citing steadier operations, fewer maintenance deferrals, and improved feed availability at key brine and hard-rock sites. Industry participants estimate that consolidated mine supply could rise marginally quarter-on-quarter from Q1 levels, with most guidance clustered around low-single-digit percentage gains. Some companies also noted that spodumene and hydroxide conversion constraints remain a limiting factor for units tied to refining rather than extraction. For the period ending 2026-06-30, buyers will be watching whether higher shipments offset ongoing restart timelines at select processing lines. Prices have been volatile since early March, and traders said the updated Q2 volumes may have limited impact unless additional lifts are confirmed beyond the current guidance range.
8951f6a9812690937b1f:
Nickel miners in Indonesia are facing higher costs as new nickel royalty payments feed into production expenses.  
State and local royalty calculations are increasing payments linked to ore output and sales.  
Several operators say the change will pressure cash flow, especially for projects with thin margins and high stripping and energy costs.  
Buyers and traders expect part of the increase to be passed through to concentrates and refined products, but timing may be uneven.  
The rise comes as Indonesian producers compete globally amid fluctuating nickel prices and ongoing investment needs.  
Companies are also reassessing expansion schedules to manage near-term royalty exposure.
7e1b8f087e7444ff47e0:
Copper smelter treatment charges (TCs) rose to their highest levels in several years as tightening concentrate supplies boosted bargaining power for refiners, according to market participants.

The gains reflected a combination of stronger demand for refining capacity and a narrower availability of copper concentrates for conversion into refined metal, traders said. As mines manage output variability and concentrate volumes remain constrained, refiners have faced fewer spot barrels, pushing TCs higher and lifting rates used in contracts across the industry.

Front-month and near-term TCs increased across major pricing hubs, with offers for processing in spot markets marking up meaningfully versus levels seen earlier this year. Several participants said the rise was broad-based, affecting both long-term contract negotiations and shorter-dated arrangements tied to the physical market.

“Smelters can command better terms when concentrates are tight and buyers are competing for limited supply,” one European refining executive said, adding that expectations for additional constraints later in the quarter were also supporting sentiment. The executive said that while demand for refined copper remains solid, the key driver has been the availability of feed material rather than changes in end-user consumption.

Smelter TCs are paid by concentrate buyers to refiners to cover processing costs and, in many cases, reflect perceived tightness in the supply chain. When concentrate availability is scarce, refiners typically charge more, which raises TCs even if refinery margins are pressured by energy and labor costs. Conversely, when concentrate supply is plentiful, TCs tend to fall as buyers negotiate for lower conversion fees.

In the background, warehouse movements and trade flows have also been watched for signs of how quickly concentrates are moving through the system. Traders said that logistics disruptions and longer lead times for deliveries in some regions have contributed to the tighter spot balance, while sellers of concentrates have been more selective on pricing.

Market pricing for copper has remained supported by expectations of continued deficits in refined supply in key consuming areas, though analysts said those fundamentals have not translated into uniform gains for processors. The sharp rise in TCs, they noted, points to a specific strain in concentrate sourcing rather than an across-the-board improvement in refining economics.

With contract renewals approaching in several jurisdictions, traders expect the higher TC levels to influence negotiated rates for the next seasonal cycle, provided concentrate tightness persists.
2dcc482001de2cfb9c67: Iron ore futures in Singapore rebounded after a 6% weekly decline, with the most-traded contract rising as of 20:00 local time on March 25, 2026, following earlier weakness tied to softer demand expectations. Prices had fallen for much of the week, pressured by reduced buying interest for steelmaking inputs and concerns about near-term margins for producers. The recovery came even as traders weighed ongoing signals of resilient supply from major exporters, including deliveries that continued to reach Asian ports. Market participants said the rebound reflected bargain demand and positioning adjustments ahead of the end of the month, when physical tender activity typically increases. Trading remained choppy, with volatility driven by rapid swings in China-linked steel futures and daily changes in freight and port inventory indicators. Analysts noted that while the week’s drop narrowed after the bounce, the contract was still vulnerable to any renewed deterioration in manufacturing data in China and other top consuming regions.
bfbf889b01f511123e02:
Chinese steel mills are signaling softer margins as they guide Q2 output lower.  
Several producers plan to moderate production to reduce supply pressure.  
The move comes as export competition and domestic demand show uneven strength.  
Spot prices have failed to sustain recent gains, squeezing profitability for blast furnace and electric-arc plants.  
Mills are also watching raw material costs, with iron ore and coking coal volatility complicating cost discipline.  
While inventory levels remain manageable, traders expect tighter operating rates if prices keep stalling.  
The guidance suggests a cautious approach through the spring demand window.  
Market participants will look for evidence that production cuts can stabilize benchmarks and limit further downside.
1eab05a9a8d4f32bf4b3:
The EU’s flagship Inflation Reduction Act-style push for electric vehicle supply chains has not yet shifted lithium markets, according to analysts tracking U.S. processing capacity, contracting and permitting timelines.

U.S. policymakers have said the IRA will accelerate domestic battery material production by boosting demand for vehicles and by offering incentives to mine, process and refine critical minerals.
